missing_ramp.title: Missing Ramp at Entrance
missing_ramp.description: No ramp provided at entrance with a level change greater than 1/2 inch.

federal_ada.title: Ramps
federal_ada.requirement: An accessible route with a ramp must be provided where there is a change in level greater than 1/2 inch. Ramps must have a running slope no steeper than 1:12 and a minimum clear width of 36 inches.

cbc_title24.title: Ramps
cbc_title24.requirement: CBC mirrors ADA 405 but has no exception for employee work areas. Ramps must comply regardless of occupancy classification.

sf_local.title: Accessible Business Entrance
sf_local.requirement: SF Ordinance 51-16 requires all primary business entrances to be accessible. Building owners must submit compliance checklists and obtain permits for ramp installation.

missing_ramp.legal_risk: Under the California Unruh Civil Rights Act, each visit to an inaccessible entrance can trigger $4,000+ in statutory damages plus attorney fees. Missing ramps are among the most commonly litigated ADA violations in California.
missing_ramp.cost_note: SF market rate for concrete ramp installation including permitting. Aluminum modular ramps may cost less ($5,000-$8,000) but may not meet SF DBI standards for permanent installations.

remediation.summary: Install ADA-compliant ramp with proper slope, width, landings, and handrails.
steps: 1. Measure existing grade change to determine required ramp run length (1:12 slope ratio)
2. Engage licensed architect or CASp to design ramp compliant with ADA 405 and CBC 11B-405
3. Apply for SF DBI building permit (alterations permit required)
4. Construct ramp with 36-inch minimum clear width, 1:12 maximum slope
5. Install handrails on both sides if rise exceeds 6 inches
6. Add level landings at top, bottom, and every 30 inches of rise
7. Install edge protection along ramp sides
8. Apply non-slip surface finish
9. Schedule final inspection with SF DBI
remediation.contractor_type: Licensed general contractor with ADA experience

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit (up to $5,000 for small businesses) and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction (up to $15,000 for any business).

visual_cues: Steps at entrance with no adjacent ramp
Level change at doorway threshold
Stairs as only means of entry
Portable or temporary ramp indicating permanent solution needed
detection.false_positive_notes: A ramp may exist at an alternate entrance. Check for directional signage to accessible entrance before flagging. Interior level changes visible through glass may not represent the primary entrance condition.

no_accessible_entrance.title: No Accessible Entrance to Building
no_accessible_entrance.description: Building lacks any accessible entrance for persons with mobility disabilities.

federal_ada.title: Accessible Entrances
federal_ada.requirement: At least 60% of all public entrances must be accessible. Accessible entrances must be connected to accessible routes and identified with the ISA if not all entrances are accessible.

cbc_title24.title: Entrances
cbc_title24.requirement: CBC requires the same 60% minimum but additionally requires that the primary entrance be accessible whenever technically feasible.

sf_local.title: Accessible Business Entrance Program
sf_local.requirement: All primary entrances to places of public accommodation must be accessible. Building owners must complete the ABE compliance process by their category deadline.

no_accessible_entrance.legal_risk: Complete lack of accessible entrance is a high-priority target for serial ADA litigation in California. Under the Unruh Civil Rights Act, plaintiffs can claim $4,000+ per occurrence. Courts have awarded damages for each separate visit to an inaccessible business.
no_accessible_entrance.cost_note: SF market rate includes ramp construction, door modifications, signage, and permitting. Complex sites with significant grade changes or structural constraints may exceed $25,000.

remediation.summary: Create at least one fully accessible entrance with ramp, compliant door, and signage.
steps: 1. Hire CASp inspector to evaluate all entrances and identify best candidate for accessible conversion
2. Engage licensed architect to design accessible entrance (ramp, door width, threshold, hardware)
3. Apply for SF DBI building permit
4. Construct accessible path from public way to entrance
5. Install or modify door to meet 32-inch clear width minimum with compliant hardware
6. Add ISA signage at accessible entrance and directional signs at non-accessible entrances
7. Ensure threshold does not exceed 1/2 inch
8. Schedule SF DBI final inspection
remediation.contractor_type: Licensed general contractor with ADA/accessibility specialization

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction. May also qualify for California state tax incentives for accessibility improvements.

visual_cues: All visible entrances have steps with no ramps
No ISA signage visible anywhere on building exterior
Narrow doorways at all entrances
Heavy manual doors with no automatic openers at any entrance
detection.false_positive_notes: An accessible entrance may exist on a different facade not visible in the photo. Look for directional signage indicating an accessible entrance elsewhere. Rear or side entrances may be accessible.

step_only_entrance.title: Steps-Only Entrance
step_only_entrance.description: Entrance accessible only via steps with no ramp, lift, or alternative accessible route.

federal_ada.title: Thresholds at Doorways
federal_ada.requirement: Thresholds at doorways must not exceed 1/2 inch in height (3/4 inch for sliding doors). Changes in level greater than 1/2 inch require a ramp or other accessible means of entry.

cbc_title24.title: Thresholds at Doorways
cbc_title24.requirement: Thresholds at doorways shall be 1/2 inch maximum in height. Raised thresholds and changes in level at doorways shall be identified with a visually contrasting stripe.

sf_local.title: Accessible Business Entrance
sf_local.requirement: Primary business entrances must provide step-free access. Property owners must remediate step-only entrances per the ABE compliance timeline.

step_only_entrance.legal_risk: Step-only entrances completely exclude wheelchair users and are a primary target for Unruh Act litigation ($4,000+ per occurrence). In California, plaintiffs' attorneys actively survey commercial districts for this violation.
step_only_entrance.cost_note: SF market rate for adding ramp or platform lift at stepped entrance. Single-step entrances may cost as little as $3,000 for a small ramp; multi-step entrances requiring platform lifts can exceed $20,000.

remediation.summary: Add ramp, platform lift, or re-grade entrance to eliminate steps as the only means of access.
steps: 1. Measure step height and available space for ramp run
2. Determine solution: ramp (preferred), platform lift, or re-grading
3. If ramp: ensure 1:12 slope ratio with adequate landing space
4. If platform lift: select model meeting ASME A18.1 standards
5. Apply for SF DBI permit (building permit for ramp; elevator permit for lift)
6. Construct solution with compliant handrails and edge protection
7. Install directional signage if accessible route differs from main entrance path
8. Schedule final inspection
remediation.contractor_type: Licensed general contractor; elevator contractor if platform lift
remediation.permit_type: SF DBI Building Permit or Elevator Permit

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: One or more steps at entrance with no ramp visible
Stairs leading to door with no alternative path
Small step or raised threshold at doorway
Portable ramp leaning against wall (indicates awareness but no permanent fix)
common_contexts: Entrances with single step that owners overlook
detection.false_positive_notes: A single step less than 1/2 inch may be a threshold issue rather than a step-only entrance. Verify the height of the level change. Also check if a ramp exists out of frame.

narrow_doorway.title: Narrow Doorway
narrow_doorway.description: Door clear opening width is less than the required 32-inch minimum.

federal_ada.title: Clear Width
federal_ada.requirement: Doorways must provide a minimum clear opening width of 32 inches, measured between the face of the door and the opposite stop when the door is open 90 degrees.

cbc_title24.title: Clear Width
cbc_title24.requirement: Same 32-inch minimum clear width requirement. CBC additionally requires that the clear opening width be maintained for the full height of the doorway.

narrow_doorway.legal_risk: Narrow doorways that prevent wheelchair access are a frequent basis for Unruh Act claims ($4,000+ per occurrence). This is particularly litigated for restroom doors and primary entrances in San Francisco.
narrow_doorway.cost_note: SF market rate for door widening including frame replacement and finish work. Load-bearing walls may increase cost to $5,000+. Includes SF DBI permit fees.

remediation.summary: Widen doorway to achieve minimum 32-inch clear opening width.
steps: 1. Measure existing clear opening width with door open 90 degrees
2. Determine if wall is load-bearing (structural engineer may be needed)
3. Apply for SF DBI alterations permit
4. Remove existing door frame and enlarge opening
5. Install new frame and door providing 32-inch minimum clear width (36-inch door leaf recommended)
6. Install compliant hardware (lever handles, not knobs)
7. Repair wall finishes and ensure threshold compliance
8. Schedule inspection

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Visibly narrow door opening
Door appears significantly narrower than standard width
Person or wheelchair would clearly not fit through opening
Non-standard or residential-size door in commercial setting
common_contexts: Older buildings with original narrow doorframes
Storage areas converted to public spaces
detection.false_positive_notes: Standard residential doors (30 inches) may appear narrow but could meet requirements if the door opens fully. The 32-inch measurement is of the clear opening, not the door leaf width. A 36-inch door leaf typically provides 32+ inches of clear width.

round_door_knob.title: Non-Compliant Door Hardware (Round Knob)
round_door_knob.description: Door has round knob hardware requiring tight grasping and wrist twisting to operate.

federal_ada.title: Door and Gate Hardware
federal_ada.requirement: Handles, pulls, latches, locks, and other operable parts on doors must be operable with one hand and not require tight grasping, pinching, or twisting of the wrist. Lever handles, push-type, and U-shaped handles are compliant. Round knobs are not.

cbc_title24.title: Door Hardware
cbc_title24.requirement: Same requirement as ADA. Hardware shall have a shape that is easy to grasp with one hand and does not require tight grasping, pinching, or twisting of the wrist to operate.

round_door_knob.legal_risk: Non-compliant door hardware is a common, easily documented violation. Under the Unruh Act, each door with a round knob can constitute a separate violation ($4,000+ each). Plaintiffs often cite multiple doors in a single lawsuit.
round_door_knob.cost_note: SF market rate for commercial-grade lever handle replacement including labor. High-security or specialty locks may cost more. No permit typically required for hardware-only replacement.

remediation.summary: Replace round knobs with lever handles or other ADA-compliant hardware.
steps: 1. Inventory all doors with non-compliant hardware
2. Select lever-style handles compatible with existing lock mechanisms
3. Replace round knobs with lever handles
4. Verify hardware operates with one hand without tight grasping or twisting
5. Ensure mounting height is 34-48 inches above finished floor

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction. Low cost makes this an excellent quick-win for compliance.

visual_cues: Round door knob visible on door
Spherical handle shape
Knob-style hardware on commercial doors
Twist-type lock mechanisms
common_contexts: Older commercial buildings with original hardware
Storage or utility room doors open to public
detection.false_positive_notes: Some round-looking handles may actually be compliant push-pull or paddle-style hardware. Look carefully at the grip mechanism. Decorative round rosettes with lever handles beneath are compliant.

missing_automatic_door.title: Missing Automatic Door Opener
missing_automatic_door.description: High-traffic public entrance lacks automatic door opener where one is required by California code.

federal_ada.title: Automatic and Power-Assisted Doors
federal_ada.requirement: ADA does not universally require automatic doors but sets standards for them when installed. Automatic doors must comply with BHMA A156.10 and remain open for a minimum of 20 seconds.

cbc_title24.title: Automatic Door Openers
cbc_title24.requirement: CBC requires power-assisted or automatic doors at primary entrances to buildings with high public traffic (medical facilities, government buildings, certain commercial occupancies). Door openers must have accessible activation controls.

sf_local.title: Accessible Business Entrance
sf_local.requirement: SF ABE program may require automatic door openers as part of accessible entrance compliance for qualifying businesses.

missing_automatic_door.legal_risk: While ADA does not universally mandate automatic doors, CBC requirements are stricter. Under the Unruh Act, failure to meet CBC standards can result in $4,000+ per occurrence in statutory damages.
missing_automatic_door.cost_note: SF market rate for commercial automatic door operator installation including electrical work and activation controls. Includes SF DBI permit fees for electrical work.

remediation.summary: Install automatic or power-assisted door operator with accessible activation controls.
steps: 1. Evaluate door type and determine compatible automatic opener system
2. Select operator compliant with BHMA A156.19 for power-assisted doors
3. Apply for SF DBI electrical permit
4. Install door operator with push-button or motion-sensor activation
5. Mount activation button at 34-48 inches above floor, within reach range
6. Adjust opening speed and hold-open time (minimum 20 seconds)
7. Test safety sensors and breakout features
8. Schedule electrical inspection
remediation.contractor_type: Automatic door installer or licensed electrician

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Heavy manual door at primary entrance
No push-button or sensor visible near door
No automatic door operator hardware visible on door frame
People struggling to open heavy entrance door
detection.false_positive_notes: Not all buildings require automatic doors under ADA. Check the building type — this is primarily a CBC requirement for certain occupancies. An existing push-button may be hidden from the camera angle.

insufficient_door_clearance.title: Insufficient Door Maneuvering Clearance
insufficient_door_clearance.description: Inadequate floor space on one or both sides of door for wheelchair maneuvering.

federal_ada.title: Maneuvering Clearances
federal_ada.requirement: Minimum maneuvering clearances at doors must be provided based on approach direction and door type. Front approach pull side requires 60 inches depth and 18 inches on latch side.

cbc_title24.title: Maneuvering Clearances
cbc_title24.requirement: Same maneuvering clearance requirements as ADA. CBC additionally requires that maneuvering clearances be level (1:48 maximum slope) and free of obstructions.

insufficient_door_clearance.legal_risk: Insufficient maneuvering clearance effectively prevents independent wheelchair access through doors. Unruh Act statutory damages of $4,000+ per occurrence apply. This is often cited alongside other door-related violations.
insufficient_door_clearance.cost_note: SF market rate for modifying wall or floor layout to achieve maneuvering clearance. May involve relocating walls, removing built-in furniture, or re-grading floor areas.

remediation.summary: Modify surrounding area to provide required maneuvering clearance at door.
steps: 1. Measure existing clearances on both sides of door for each approach direction
2. Identify which clearance dimension is deficient
3. Determine solution: relocate obstructions, remove built-ins, widen corridor, or change door swing
4. If structural changes needed, apply for SF DBI alterations permit
5. Execute modifications to achieve required clearances
6. Verify clearance is level (1:48 maximum slope)
7. Consider adding automatic door opener if clearance cannot be fully achieved

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Walls, furniture, or fixtures very close to door on latch side
Narrow hallway or alcove around door
Objects blocking floor space near door
Door opens into cramped space
common_contexts: Doors at end of narrow corridors
detection.false_positive_notes: Required clearance varies by approach direction (front, side, latch side). A tight-looking space may actually meet requirements for the applicable approach. Automatic doors have reduced clearance requirements.

excessive_door_force.title: Excessive Door Opening Force
excessive_door_force.description: Interior door requires more than 5 pounds of force to open.

federal_ada.title: Door and Gate Opening Force
federal_ada.requirement: Interior hinged doors must require no more than 5 pounds of force to open. Fire doors may have higher force per local fire code but must have minimum force necessary. Sliding and folding doors: 5 lbs maximum.

cbc_title24.title: Door and Gate Opening Force
cbc_title24.requirement: Same 5 lbs maximum for interior doors. CBC also specifies that exterior doors shall have the minimum opening force allowable by the applicable fire code. Door closers must be adjusted to the minimum force necessary.

excessive_door_force.legal_risk: Excessive door force prevents access for persons with limited upper body strength. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ statutory damages per occurrence. This is a measurable, objective standard easily proven in court.
excessive_door_force.cost_note: SF market rate for door closer adjustment or replacement. Simple adjustment may cost as little as $100. New commercial closer installation runs $250-$500.

remediation.summary: Adjust or replace door closer to reduce opening force to 5 pounds or less.
steps: 1. Measure door opening force with a door pressure gauge
2. Adjust existing door closer tension to achieve 5 lbs or less
3. If adjustment insufficient, replace door closer with adjustable commercial model
4. For fire doors, consult with fire marshal on minimum acceptable force
5. Re-measure force after adjustment to verify compliance
6. Consider power-assist door operator for doors that cannot meet 5 lb requirement
remediation.contractor_type: Door hardware specialist or general handyman

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction. Very low cost quick-win.

visual_cues: Heavy-looking commercial door closer mechanism
Person visibly struggling to push door open
Fire-rated door with heavy closure hardware
Self-closing door that slams shut quickly
detection.false_positive_notes: Cannot accurately assess door force from a photo alone. This detection relies on visual cues like heavy closer hardware. Fire doors may legitimately require higher force — check with fire code requirements. Exterior doors are exempt from the 5 lb requirement.

steep_ramp.title: Excessive Ramp Slope
steep_ramp.description: Ramp running slope exceeds the maximum 1:12 (8.33%) ratio.

federal_ada.title: Ramp Slope
federal_ada.requirement: Ramp running slope must not exceed 1:12 (8.33%). Cross slope must not exceed 1:48 (2.08%). Rise for any single ramp run must be 30 inches maximum.

cbc_title24.title: Slope
cbc_title24.requirement: Same 1:12 maximum running slope. CBC does not allow the ADA exception for existing sites to use steeper slopes (1:10 for 6-inch max rise or 1:8 for 3-inch max rise), making CBC stricter for existing buildings.

steep_ramp.legal_risk: Steep ramps create safety hazards and inaccessibility for wheelchair users. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. A ramp that exists but doesn't comply is still a violation — not a defense against litigation.
steep_ramp.cost_note: SF market rate for demolishing and rebuilding a ramp to proper slope. Includes new concrete work, handrails, and permitting. May be higher if site conditions require retaining walls or significant grading.

remediation.summary: Rebuild ramp to achieve maximum 1:12 running slope with compliant landings.
steps: 1. Measure existing ramp slope with digital level or smart level app
2. Calculate required ramp run length based on total rise (rise x 12 = minimum run)
3. Engage architect to redesign ramp if run length requires switchback configuration
4. Apply for SF DBI alterations permit
5. Demolish existing non-compliant ramp
6. Construct new ramp with 1:12 maximum slope and 1:48 maximum cross slope
7. Install compliant handrails, landings, and edge protection
8. Apply non-slip surface treatment
9. Schedule final inspection
remediation.contractor_type: Licensed general contractor with concrete/accessibility experience

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Ramp that appears visually steep
Short ramp covering significant height change
Ramp surface that looks like it exceeds 8% grade
Wheelchair user would need significant effort or assistance
common_contexts: Retrofit ramps added without proper design
Ramps on steep San Francisco streets
detection.false_positive_notes: Visual assessment of slope can be misleading, especially on San Francisco hills where surrounding terrain skews perception. A ramp on a steep street may appear steeper than it is relative to the building floor. Measurement is essential.

missing_handrail.title: Missing Handrails on Ramp
missing_handrail.description: Ramp with a rise greater than 6 inches lacks required handrails on both sides.

federal_ada.title: Handrails
federal_ada.requirement: Ramps with a rise greater than 6 inches must have handrails on both sides. Handrails must be 34-38 inches above the ramp surface and comply with Section 505.

cbc_title24.title: Handrails
cbc_title24.requirement: Same requirement as ADA. CBC is stricter in that it does not exempt employee work areas and requires handrails on all ramps with rise greater than 6 inches regardless of location.

missing_handrail.legal_risk: Missing handrails create fall hazards and prevent safe ramp use. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Combined with steep ramp violations, damages can multiply.
missing_handrail.cost_note: SF market rate for commercial handrail installation on one side of a ramp. Includes materials (stainless steel or aluminum), mounting hardware, and labor. Both sides typically needed ($4,000 total).

remediation.summary: Install handrails on both sides of the ramp compliant with ADA 505 and CBC 11B-505.
steps: 1. Measure ramp rise to confirm handrails are required (rise > 6 inches)
2. Select handrail material and profile compliant with gripping requirements (1.25-2 inch diameter)
3. Install handrails on both sides at 34-38 inches above ramp surface
4. Extend handrails 12 inches minimum beyond top and bottom of ramp
5. Ensure handrails are continuous and return to wall, post, or floor at ends
6. Verify handrails can withstand 250 lbs of force
7. No permit typically required for handrail-only installation on existing ramp
remediation.contractor_type: Metal fabricator or licensed general contractor

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Ramp with no handrails on either side
Ramp with handrail on only one side
Ramp with non-graspable top rail only (no separate handrail)
Short ramp that clearly rises more than 6 inches without rails
common_contexts: Retrofit ramps added without full compliance
Parking structure ramps to building entries
detection.false_positive_notes: Ramps with less than 6 inches of rise are exempt from handrail requirements. Curb ramps are also exempt. A guard rail or decorative railing may not meet graspability requirements even if present.

missing_ramp_landing.title: Missing Ramp Landing
missing_ramp_landing.description: Ramp lacks required level landings at top, bottom, or at changes in direction.

federal_ada.title: Landings
federal_ada.requirement: Ramps must have landings at the top and bottom of each ramp run. Landings must be at least 60 inches long and as wide as the ramp. Where ramps change direction, the landing must be 60 x 60 inches minimum.

cbc_title24.title: Landings
cbc_title24.requirement: Same 60-inch minimum landing length. CBC additionally requires that landings be level with a maximum slope of 1:48 in any direction.

missing_ramp_landing.legal_risk: Missing landings create safety hazards where wheelchair users cannot safely stop or turn. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Often cited alongside slope and handrail violations.
missing_ramp_landing.cost_note: SF market rate for adding a level landing to an existing ramp. Includes concrete work, potential ramp reconfiguration, and finishing. Cost increases significantly if ramp must be extended to accommodate landing.

remediation.summary: Add level landings at top, bottom, and direction changes of ramp.
steps: 1. Identify all locations requiring landings (top, bottom, direction changes, every 30 inches of rise)
2. Design landing areas 60 inches minimum in length and ramp width
3. Apply for SF DBI permit if structural modifications needed
4. Construct level landing platforms (1:48 maximum slope)
5. Integrate landings with existing ramp structure
6. Ensure continuous handrails through landing areas
7. Apply non-slip surface treatment to landings

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Ramp that transitions directly to floor or ground without flat area
Ramp that changes direction without level platform
Long ramp run without intermediate flat sections
Door opening directly onto sloped ramp surface
detection.false_positive_notes: Short ramps with less than 6 inches of rise may have reduced landing requirements. The landing must be truly level (1:48 max) — a slightly sloped area may look flat but not comply.

missing_edge_protection.title: Missing Ramp Edge Protection
missing_edge_protection.description: Ramp sides lack curbs, walls, or barriers to prevent wheelchairs from rolling off edges.

federal_ada.title: Edge Protection
federal_ada.requirement: Edge protection must be provided on each side of ramp runs and at each side of ramp landings. Options include curbs, walls, railings, or extended floor surfaces.

cbc_title24.title: Edge Protection
cbc_title24.requirement: CBC requires either a curb or a barrier for edge protection. CBC does not recognize the extended floor or ground surface option permitted by ADA, making CBC stricter.

missing_edge_protection.legal_risk: Missing edge protection creates fall hazards for wheelchair users. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. If an injury results from missing edge protection, premises liability claims may apply in addition.
missing_edge_protection.cost_note: SF market rate for installing curb edges or barriers on both sides of a ramp. Includes materials and labor for concrete curbs or metal barriers.

remediation.summary: Install curbs, walls, or barriers along ramp edges to prevent wheelchair rolloff.
steps: 1. Assess existing ramp edges for protection gaps
2. Select edge protection type: 4-inch minimum curb (preferred in CBC), wall extension, or railing with bottom rail
3. Install edge protection on both sides of ramp run and landings
4. Ensure edge protection extends full length of ramp
5. Verify curb height is 4 inches minimum above ramp surface
remediation.contractor_type: Licensed general contractor or metal fabricator

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Ramp with open edges and no curbs or barriers
Ramp surface that drops off at sides
Gap between ramp edge and adjacent surface
Ramp without bottom rail or curb along sides
common_contexts: Ramps adjacent to landscaping or grade changes
detection.false_positive_notes: A ramp with walls on both sides inherently has edge protection. Ramps at grade (flush with surrounding surface) may not need edge protection. Check if the ramp edge is actually elevated above adjacent ground.

missing_handrail_extensions.title: Missing Handrail Extensions
missing_handrail_extensions.description: Handrails on ramp do not extend the required 12 inches beyond top and bottom of ramp run.

federal_ada.title: Handrail Extensions
federal_ada.requirement: Handrails must extend horizontally 12 inches minimum beyond the top and bottom of ramp runs. Extensions must return to a wall, post, or floor, or be continuous to handrail of adjacent ramp run.

cbc_title24.title: Handrail Extensions
cbc_title24.requirement: Same 12-inch extension requirement. CBC 11B-505.10.3 has stricter requirements for bottom extensions at stairs than ADA.

missing_handrail_extensions.legal_risk: While less severe than missing handrails entirely, non-compliant extensions are still actionable under the Unruh Act ($4,000+ per occurrence). Often cited as part of a bundle of ramp violations.
missing_handrail_extensions.cost_note: SF market rate for modifying existing handrails to add compliant extensions on both sides. Includes material, fabrication, and installation.

remediation.summary: Extend existing handrails 12 inches beyond top and bottom of ramp with proper returns.
steps: 1. Measure existing handrail termination points
2. Fabricate or obtain extension pieces matching existing handrail profile
3. Extend handrails 12 inches horizontally beyond top and bottom of ramp
4. Add returns to wall, post, or floor at extension ends
5. Ensure extensions do not create a hazard in the circulation path
6. Verify continuous gripping surface through extension

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Handrails that end abruptly at top or bottom of ramp
Handrails that do not extend past the ramp slope
Handrail ends that are not returned to wall or floor
Handrails cut flush with end of ramp
common_contexts: Handrails that terminate at wall corners
Ramps where space constraints limited handrail installation
detection.false_positive_notes: Extensions may be present but not clearly visible in the photo angle. Handrails that return to a wall immediately at the ramp end may still comply if the return is at the correct location.

excessive_ramp_rise.title: Excessive Ramp Rise Without Landing
excessive_ramp_rise.description: Single ramp run exceeds 30 inches of rise without an intermediate landing.

federal_ada.title: Rise
federal_ada.requirement: The rise for any single ramp run must not exceed 30 inches (760 mm). Ramps with greater total rise must have intermediate landings.

cbc_title24.title: Rise
cbc_title24.requirement: Same 30-inch maximum rise per run. At 1:12 slope, this means a maximum run length of 30 feet before a landing is required.

excessive_ramp_rise.legal_risk: Excessive continuous rise creates fatigue and safety hazards for wheelchair users. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Often combined with missing landing violations.
excessive_ramp_rise.cost_note: SF market rate for reconfiguring a ramp to add intermediate landings. May require significant reconstruction including switchback design if space is limited.

remediation.summary: Add intermediate landings to break ramp into runs of 30 inches or less rise each.
steps: 1. Measure total rise of existing ramp
2. Calculate number of intermediate landings needed (one per 30 inches of rise)
3. Design ramp configuration with landings (may require switchback layout)
4. Apply for SF DBI alterations permit
5. Reconstruct ramp with compliant runs and 60-inch minimum landings
6. Install or modify handrails for continuous run through landings
7. Schedule final inspection

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Very long ramp run without any flat sections
Ramp that covers more than approximately 2.5 feet of height in one run
Ramp that appears to go on for more than 30 feet without a break
Switchback ramp with runs that appear excessively long
detection.false_positive_notes: Estimating 30 inches of rise from a photo is difficult. A ramp that looks long may have a gentle slope and not exceed 30 inches of rise. Measurement or dimensional reference in the photo is needed.

missing_accessible_parking_sign.title: Missing Accessible Parking Sign
missing_accessible_parking_sign.description: Accessible parking space lacks required International Symbol of Accessibility signage.

federal_ada.title: Identification
federal_ada.requirement: Accessible parking spaces must be identified by signs with the International Symbol of Accessibility (ISA). Signs must be 60 inches minimum above the ground, measured to the bottom of the sign.

cbc_title24.title: Identification
cbc_title24.requirement: CBC requires ISA signs at 80 inches minimum above ground (stricter than ADA's 60 inches). Signs must include 'Minimum Fine $250' text and tow-away warning per California Vehicle Code 22511.8.

missing_accessible_parking_sign.legal_risk: Missing signage is a clearly documented, easily photographed violation. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Each unmarked space can be a separate violation. Parking violations are among the most frequently litigated ADA claims in California.
missing_accessible_parking_sign.cost_note: SF market rate for California-compliant accessible parking sign including post installation. Must include ISA symbol, fine warning, and tow-away text per CBC requirements.

remediation.summary: Install California-compliant ISA parking sign at each accessible space.
steps: 1. Purchase California-compliant accessible parking signs (ISA symbol, 'Minimum Fine $250', tow-away warning)
2. Install sign post at each accessible parking space
3. Mount sign at 80 inches minimum above ground (CBC requirement, stricter than ADA's 60 inches)
4. Add 'Van Accessible' designation to van-accessible spaces
5. Ensure signs are visible and not obstructed

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction. Very low cost quick-win.

visual_cues: Blue-painted parking space without sign post
Accessible space markings on pavement but no vertical sign
Sign post without ISA sign attached
Low-mounted sign below 60 inches
detection.false_positive_notes: A sign may exist but be obscured by trees, vehicles, or structures. Check for signs mounted on walls or overhead. Some jurisdictions use pavement markings in addition to signs but pavement markings alone do not meet the requirement.

missing_parking_striping.title: Missing Parking Access Aisle Striping
missing_parking_striping.description: Accessible parking space lacks required painted access aisle markings.

federal_ada.title: Access Aisle
federal_ada.requirement: Access aisles must be marked to discourage parking in them. Standard access aisles must be 60 inches wide minimum. Van-accessible aisles must be 96 inches wide minimum.

cbc_title24.title: Access Aisle
cbc_title24.requirement: CBC requires access aisles with blue border striping and diagonal hatch marks. 'NO PARKING' must be painted within the access aisle. Van-accessible aisles must be 96 inches minimum width.

missing_parking_striping.legal_risk: Unmarked access aisles lead to parking encroachment that blocks wheelchair access. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Easily documented and photographed violation.
missing_parking_striping.cost_note: SF market rate for professional parking lot striping including blue border, diagonal hatch marks, ISA symbol, and 'NO PARKING' text. Includes surface preparation and traffic-rated paint.

remediation.summary: Paint compliant access aisle striping adjacent to accessible parking spaces.
steps: 1. Clean and prepare pavement surface
2. Layout access aisle dimensions (60 inches minimum; 96 inches for van-accessible)
3. Paint blue border lines around access aisle perimeter
4. Paint diagonal blue hatch marks within access aisle
5. Stencil 'NO PARKING' in white within the access aisle
6. Paint ISA symbol in the parking space
7. Allow paint to cure before opening to traffic

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction. Low cost, quick implementation.

visual_cues: Accessible parking space without adjacent striped aisle
Faded or missing blue hatch marks next to accessible space
Access aisle area without 'NO PARKING' text
Cars parked in what should be the access aisle area
common_contexts: Aging parking lots with faded markings
detection.false_positive_notes: Faded but still visible striping may technically comply. New asphalt may be awaiting restriping. Some lots use alternative marking methods (raised markers, different paint colors) that may be compliant.

insufficient_accessible_spaces.title: Insufficient Accessible Parking Spaces
insufficient_accessible_spaces.description: Parking facility does not provide the required minimum number of accessible spaces based on total capacity.

cbc_title24.title: Minimum Number
cbc_title24.requirement: CBC follows the same ratio table as ADA. However, CBC requires additional accessible spaces for medical facilities and outpatient units (10% of total must be accessible).

insufficient_accessible_spaces.legal_risk: Insufficient accessible parking is a systemic violation affecting every visitor. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence per visit. Parking violations are the single most litigated category of ADA claims in California.
insufficient_accessible_spaces.cost_note: SF market rate for converting a standard parking space to accessible, including restriping, signage, and access aisle. Does not include surface repairs if pavement is damaged.

remediation.summary: Add accessible parking spaces to meet required minimum based on total lot capacity.
steps: 1. Count total parking spaces in the facility
2. Calculate required number of accessible spaces per ADA 208.2 table
3. Identify optimal locations closest to accessible building entrance
4. Restripe selected spaces with accessible parking markings and access aisles
5. Install California-compliant signage at each new accessible space
6. Ensure accessible route from parking to building entrance
7. Designate required van-accessible spaces (1 per 6 accessible spaces)
remediation.contractor_type: Parking lot striping contractor and sign installer

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Large parking lot with very few or no visible accessible spaces
Accessible spaces far from building entrance
Parking lot with no blue-painted spaces visible
Only one accessible space in a large commercial lot
detection.false_positive_notes: May not be able to count total spaces from a single photo. Some accessible spaces may be out of frame. Multi-level garages may have accessible spaces on different levels. Count must include the entire facility, not just what's visible.

missing_van_accessible_space.title: Missing Van-Accessible Parking Space
missing_van_accessible_space.description: No van-accessible parking space with 96-inch wide access aisle and 98-inch minimum vertical clearance.

federal_ada.title: Vehicle Spaces
federal_ada.requirement: At least one of every six accessible parking spaces (or fraction thereof) must be van-accessible with a minimum 132-inch wide space or standard space with 96-inch access aisle, and 98-inch minimum vertical clearance.

cbc_title24.title: Vehicle Spaces
cbc_title24.requirement: Same van-accessible ratios as ADA. CBC additionally requires that van-accessible spaces be located on the shortest accessible route to the building entrance.

missing_van_accessible_space.legal_risk: Missing van-accessible spaces exclude wheelchair van users entirely. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Van users are often the most motivated and organized plaintiffs in ADA litigation.
missing_van_accessible_space.cost_note: SF market rate for converting a standard accessible space to van-accessible, including wider access aisle restriping, 'Van Accessible' signage, and vertical clearance verification.

remediation.summary: Designate and mark at least one van-accessible space with wider access aisle and appropriate signage.
steps: 1. Calculate required number of van-accessible spaces (1 per 6 accessible spaces)
2. Select location closest to accessible entrance with adequate vertical clearance (98 inches minimum)
3. Restripe space with 96-inch minimum access aisle
4. Install 'Van Accessible' sign below ISA sign
5. Verify 98-inch minimum vertical clearance on route from entry to space
6. Ensure accessible route from space to building entrance
remediation.contractor_type: Parking lot striping contractor and sign installer

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Accessible parking spaces with standard-width (60-inch) access aisles only
No 'Van Accessible' signage visible at any accessible space
Parking structure with low clearance at accessible spaces
All accessible spaces appear to be standard width
common_contexts: Small parking lots with minimal accessible spaces
Older facilities that predate van-accessible requirements
detection.false_positive_notes: Van-accessible spaces may exist in a different area of the lot not visible in the photo. Vertical clearance issues are common in garages but may not be obvious from exterior photos.

damaged_parking_surface.title: Damaged Accessible Parking Surface
damaged_parking_surface.description: Accessible parking space or access aisle has surface cracks, heaving, or deterioration creating barriers.

federal_ada.title: Floor or Ground Surfaces
federal_ada.requirement: Parking spaces and access aisles must have surface slopes not steeper than 1:48 in all directions. Surfaces must be stable, firm, and slip-resistant per Section 302.

cbc_title24.title: Floor or Ground Surfaces
cbc_title24.requirement: Same slope and surface requirements as ADA. CBC requires that surfaces be maintained to remain compliant — deterioration that creates level changes or unstable surfaces is a violation.

damaged_parking_surface.legal_risk: Damaged surfaces can trap wheelchair casters, cause falls, and prevent van lift deployment. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Maintenance failures are not a defense — ongoing compliance is required.
damaged_parking_surface.cost_note: SF market rate for resurfacing an accessible parking space and access aisle. Includes removal of damaged surface, base repair, new asphalt or concrete, and restriping.

remediation.summary: Repair or resurface accessible parking space and access aisle to provide stable, level surface.
steps: 1. Assess extent of surface damage (cracks, heaving, potholes, settlement)
2. Determine repair method: patching, overlay, or full replacement
3. Remove damaged surface material
4. Repair base course if needed
5. Install new surface ensuring 1:48 maximum slope in all directions
6. Restripe accessible parking markings and access aisle
7. Allow proper cure time before opening to traffic

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Visible cracks or heaving in accessible parking area
Pothole or settlement in access aisle
Crumbling pavement edges around accessible space
Standing water indicating uneven surface
Tree root damage lifting pavement
common_contexts: Spaces near trees with invasive roots
detection.false_positive_notes: Minor surface imperfections (hairline cracks, small texture variations) may not constitute a violation. The key question is whether the surface creates level changes greater than 1/4 inch or is unstable for wheelchair use.

blocked_accessible_route.title: Blocked Accessible Route
blocked_accessible_route.description: Obstruction in an accessible route prevents clear passage for wheelchair users.

federal_ada.title: Accessible Routes
federal_ada.requirement: Accessible routes must be continuous, unobstructed paths connecting all accessible elements and spaces. Minimum clear width of 36 inches (44 inches for corridors with frequent use).

cbc_title24.title: Accessible Routes
cbc_title24.requirement: Same continuous path requirements. CBC additionally requires that accessible routes be maintained free of obstructions at all times, including temporary obstructions.

sf_local.title: Sidewalk Maintenance
sf_local.requirement: Property owners are responsible for maintaining sidewalks and accessible routes adjacent to their property free from obstructions per SF Public Works Code Article 706.

blocked_accessible_route.legal_risk: Blocked routes completely prevent access. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence per visit. Even temporary obstructions (merchandise displays, A-frame signs) can trigger liability if they are recurring.
blocked_accessible_route.cost_note: SF market rate for removing or relocating obstructions. Cost varies widely from near-zero (moving a sign) to several thousand (relocating permanent fixtures). Ongoing management may require staff training.

remediation.summary: Remove or relocate obstructions to restore minimum 36-inch clear width along accessible route.
steps: 1. Identify all obstructions along accessible route (furniture, signs, displays, planters, equipment)
2. Relocate movable items outside the accessible path of travel
3. For permanent obstructions, evaluate relocation or rerouting options
4. Mark accessible route boundaries if needed to prevent future encroachment
5. Train staff on maintaining clear accessible routes
6. Establish regular inspection schedule to prevent recurrence
remediation.contractor_type: Business owner or general contractor for permanent fixtures
remediation.timeline: 1-3 days for movable obstructions; 1-4 weeks for permanent fixtures

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction if permanent modifications are made.

visual_cues: Objects blocking sidewalk or pathway
Merchandise displays narrowing walkway
A-frame signs in middle of sidewalk
Planters or furniture reducing clear width below 36 inches
Construction materials or equipment in path
Parked bicycles or scooters blocking route
common_contexts: Building entries with deliveries or trash bins
detection.false_positive_notes: A route may appear blocked but still maintain 36 inches of clear width. Multiple accessible routes may exist — blocking one route is still a violation only if it is a designated accessible route. Temporary construction with proper detour signage may be compliant.

missing_curb_cut.title: Missing Curb Ramp
missing_curb_cut.description: No curb ramp provided where an accessible route crosses a curb.

federal_ada.title: Curb Ramps
federal_ada.requirement: Curb ramps must be provided wherever an accessible route crosses a curb. Maximum running slope of 1:12. Must have detectable warning surfaces (truncated domes). Flared sides with maximum 1:10 slope.

cbc_title24.title: Curb Ramps
cbc_title24.requirement: Same requirements as ADA. CBC additionally requires that curb ramps have a 48-inch minimum landing at the top and be aligned with the crosswalk direction.

sf_local.title: Curb Ramp Program
sf_local.requirement: SF Department of Public Works manages curb ramp installation citywide. Property owners can request curb ramps through 311 or DPW. SF has allocated $86M over 10 years for ADA curb ramp installation.

missing_curb_cut.legal_risk: Missing curb cuts strand wheelchair users at intersections. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Municipalities and property owners can both be liable. SF has faced class-action lawsuits over missing curb ramps.
missing_curb_cut.cost_note: SF market rate for concrete curb ramp installation including detectable warning surface, drainage modifications, and permitting. SF DPW installations may be funded through the city's ADA transition plan.

remediation.summary: Install compliant curb ramp with detectable warnings where accessible route meets curb.
steps: 1. Determine if curb ramp is responsibility of property owner or SF DPW (public right-of-way)
2. If public ROW, request curb ramp through SF 311 or DPW website
3. If private property, engage civil engineer to design compliant curb ramp
4. Apply for SF DPW encroachment permit (public) or SF DBI permit (private)
5. Saw-cut and remove existing curb section
6. Construct curb ramp with 1:12 maximum slope and flared sides
7. Install truncated dome detectable warning surface
8. Ensure proper drainage
9. Schedule inspection
remediation.permit_type: SF DPW Encroachment Permit or SF DBI Permit

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction for private property. Public ROW curb ramps may be city-funded.

visual_cues: Curb with no ramp at pedestrian crossing
Sidewalk ending at street level with vertical curb face
Wheelchair user would need to navigate off curb into street
Intersection corner without visible curb cut
common_contexts: Driveway entrances used as pedestrian routes
detection.false_positive_notes: A curb ramp may exist at a nearby location (adjacent corner or mid-block). Driveway aprons are sometimes used as informal curb ramps but may not meet ADA standards. Some modern curbs are flush with the street, eliminating the need for a ramp.

surface_gaps_or_cracks.title: Surface Gaps or Cracks in Accessible Route
surface_gaps_or_cracks.description: Openings or gaps in the walking surface exceed 1/2 inch, creating barriers for wheelchair users.

federal_ada.title: Openings
federal_ada.requirement: Openings in floor or ground surfaces shall not allow passage of a sphere more than 1/2 inch (13 mm) in diameter. Elongated openings must be placed perpendicular to the dominant direction of travel.

cbc_title24.title: Openings
cbc_title24.requirement: Same 1/2-inch maximum opening size. CBC additionally requires that grate openings in accessible routes be no greater than 1/2 inch in the direction of travel.

sf_local.title: Sidewalk Maintenance
sf_local.requirement: Property owners responsible for maintaining sidewalks in safe condition. Cracks or gaps that create trip hazards or accessibility barriers must be repaired.

surface_gaps_or_cracks.legal_risk: Surface gaps trap wheelchair casters and cane tips, creating fall hazards. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Property owners are liable for sidewalk conditions adjacent to their property in San Francisco.
surface_gaps_or_cracks.cost_note: SF market rate for concrete sidewalk repair or patching. Tree root-caused damage may require root pruning or barrier installation, increasing cost. Large-area replacements can exceed $5,000.

remediation.summary: Repair or replace walking surface to eliminate gaps exceeding 1/2 inch.
steps: 1. Survey accessible route for all gaps, cracks, and openings exceeding 1/2 inch
2. For minor cracks: fill with concrete crack filler or epoxy compound
3. For larger gaps: saw-cut and replace damaged concrete sections
4. For tree root damage: coordinate with SF Bureau of Urban Forestry for root pruning
5. For grates/drains: replace with ADA-compliant grates (1/2-inch max openings perpendicular to travel)
6. Ensure repaired surface is flush with surrounding pavement (maximum 1/4-inch level change)
7. Apply for SF DPW sidewalk repair permit if in public ROW

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction for private property repairs.

visual_cues: Visible cracks wider than 1/2 inch in sidewalk or walkway
Gaps between concrete panels
Broken or displaced pavement sections
Tree root uplift causing pavement separation
Drainage grates with wide openings parallel to travel direction
common_contexts: Brick or paver walkways with loose joints
Transition points between different surface materials
detection.false_positive_notes: Narrow hairline cracks (less than 1/2 inch) do not violate this standard. Control joints in concrete are typically less than 1/2 inch and are compliant. Expansion joints with proper filler are acceptable.

excessive_cross_slope.title: Excessive Cross Slope on Accessible Route
excessive_cross_slope.description: Walking surface cross slope exceeds 1:48 (2.08%), causing wheelchair drift.

federal_ada.title: Slope
federal_ada.requirement: The cross slope of walking surfaces must not exceed 1:48 (2.08%). Running slope of accessible routes must not exceed 1:20 (5%) unless designed as a ramp.

cbc_title24.title: Slope
cbc_title24.requirement: Same 1:48 maximum cross slope. CBC does not provide exceptions for existing conditions — all accessible routes must meet this standard regardless of construction date.

excessive_cross_slope.legal_risk: Excessive cross slope causes wheelchair drift toward traffic or hazards, creating safety risks. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. On San Francisco's hilly terrain, this is a commonly encountered but often overlooked violation.
excessive_cross_slope.cost_note: SF market rate for regrading a section of walkway to correct cross slope. Cost varies significantly based on length and existing conditions. SF's hilly terrain can make corrections particularly expensive.

remediation.summary: Regrade walking surface to achieve maximum 1:48 cross slope.
steps: 1. Measure cross slope along entire accessible route using digital level
2. Identify all sections exceeding 1:48 cross slope
3. Design regrading plan maintaining positive drainage while achieving compliant slope
4. Apply for SF DPW or DBI permit as applicable
5. Remove existing surface material in non-compliant sections
6. Regrade base to achieve 1:48 maximum cross slope
7. Install new surface material
8. Verify compliance with measurements
9. Ensure drainage is maintained
remediation.permit_type: SF DPW Sidewalk Repair Permit or SF DBI Alterations Permit

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Sidewalk visibly tilting to one side
Water pooling on one side of walkway indicating slope
Walkway following a hillside with visible lateral tilt
Wheelchair user leaning to compensate for slope
common_contexts: Walkways near driveways with cross-slope transitions
detection.false_positive_notes: Cross slope is very difficult to assess visually. A walkway may appear level but exceed 1:48, or appear sloped but be within limits. Measurement with a digital level is essential. San Francisco's hilly streets can make photos misleading about actual cross slopes.

insufficient_route_width.title: Insufficient Accessible Route Width
insufficient_route_width.description: Accessible route width is less than the required 36-inch minimum.

federal_ada.title: Clear Width
federal_ada.requirement: The clear width of accessible routes must be 36 inches minimum. At 180-degree turns, the clear width must be 48 inches minimum at the turn or 36 inches minimum with a 60-inch turning space.

cbc_title24.title: Clear Width
cbc_title24.requirement: Same 36-inch minimum clear width. CBC does not allow the ADA exception for a 32-inch minimum width at constrictions up to 24 inches long.

insufficient_route_width.legal_risk: Narrow routes prevent wheelchair passage entirely. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Frequently cited in lawsuits involving interior commercial spaces with cluttered aisles.
insufficient_route_width.cost_note: SF market rate for widening an accessible route section. Cost depends on whether walls need relocation (higher) or if furniture/fixtures simply need rearranging (lower).

remediation.summary: Widen accessible route to achieve minimum 36-inch clear width throughout.
steps: 1. Measure clear width of accessible route at all points, especially narrow sections
2. Identify obstructions reducing width below 36 inches
3. Remove or relocate movable obstructions (furniture, displays, equipment)
4. For structural narrowing, evaluate wall relocation or alternative route
5. If structural work needed, apply for SF DBI alterations permit
6. Execute widening modifications
7. Mark or protect route boundaries to prevent future encroachment
remediation.contractor_type: Licensed general contractor for structural work; facility management for furniture relocation
remediation.timeline: 1 day to 4 weeks depending on scope

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Narrow corridor or walkway
Path squeezed between walls, furniture, or fixtures
Person in wheelchair would clearly not fit through passage
Display racks or merchandise narrowing aisle
detection.false_positive_notes: A 36-inch clear width is narrower than many people estimate. A standard wheelchair is about 26 inches wide. What appears narrow may actually comply. Measure the clear width between fixed elements, not including temporary items.

changes_in_level.title: Abrupt Changes in Level
changes_in_level.description: Walking surface has level changes exceeding 1/4 inch without beveling or ramping.

federal_ada.title: Changes in Level
federal_ada.requirement: Changes in level up to 1/4 inch may be vertical. Changes between 1/4 inch and 1/2 inch must be beveled at 1:2 maximum slope. Changes greater than 1/2 inch must be ramped (Section 405) or use a curb ramp (Section 406).

cbc_title24.title: Changes in Level
cbc_title24.requirement: Same thresholds as ADA. CBC additionally requires that level changes be identified with visual contrast where they occur in the accessible route.

changes_in_level.legal_risk: Abrupt level changes cause wheelchair tipping and tripping hazards. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. This is a common, easily documented violation in older buildings and sidewalks.
changes_in_level.cost_note: SF market rate for grinding, beveling, or ramping a level change. Simple grinding or beveling may cost as little as $200. Creating a small ramp section costs more.

remediation.summary: Eliminate or reduce level changes to compliant thresholds with beveling or ramping.
steps: 1. Identify and measure all level changes along the accessible route
2. For changes 1/4 to 1/2 inch: bevel the transition at 1:2 slope
3. For changes greater than 1/2 inch: install ramp or regrade surface
4. For concrete: use grinding to create beveled transitions
5. For thresholds at doors: install ADA-compliant threshold ramp
6. Apply visual contrast marking at level changes per CBC
7. Verify repaired transitions with measurements
remediation.contractor_type: Concrete contractor or general handyman for minor repairs

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Visible lip or step between surface sections
Cracked or heaved concrete creating an edge
Threshold at doorway that appears raised
Transition between floor materials with visible height difference
Sidewalk panel raised above adjacent panel
common_contexts: Transitions between indoor and outdoor surfaces
detection.false_positive_notes: Level changes of 1/4 inch or less are permitted without treatment. Beveled transitions may appear as level changes but could be compliant. Door thresholds up to 1/2 inch (3/4 inch for sliding doors) with proper bevel are acceptable.

unstable_ground_surface.title: Unstable Ground Surface on Accessible Route
unstable_ground_surface.description: Walking surface consists of loose material (gravel, sand, mulch) that is not stable, firm, or slip-resistant.

federal_ada.title: General Ground Surfaces
federal_ada.requirement: Floor or ground surfaces along accessible routes must be stable, firm, and slip-resistant. Carpet pile height must not exceed 1/2 inch.

cbc_title24.title: General
cbc_title24.requirement: Same requirement for stable, firm, slip-resistant surfaces. CBC requires surfaces to be maintained in compliant condition — deterioration over time does not excuse non-compliance.

unstable_ground_surface.legal_risk: Unstable surfaces prevent wheelchair travel and create fall hazards for mobility-impaired individuals. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Garden paths, courtyards, and outdoor dining areas are common sources of violations.
unstable_ground_surface.cost_note: SF market rate for replacing loose-surface path with firm, stable material (concrete, pavers, or compacted surface). Cost varies with area and material choice.

remediation.summary: Replace loose surface material with stable, firm, slip-resistant surface along accessible route.
steps: 1. Survey all accessible route surfaces for unstable, loose, or non-firm materials
2. Determine replacement surface material (concrete, pavers, compacted decomposed granite, rubber)
3. Remove loose surface material and prepare base
4. Install stable, firm surface material meeting ADA 302.1
5. Ensure proper drainage to prevent water pooling
6. If using compacted materials, establish maintenance schedule for re-compaction
7. Apply for permit if work is in public right-of-way

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Gravel or crushed stone pathway
Mulch or wood chip surface on route
Sand or loose dirt path
Grass or lawn used as accessible route
Deep carpet or thick mat that would impede wheels
common_contexts: Park trails designated as accessible routes
detection.false_positive_notes: Properly compacted decomposed granite (DG) with stabilizer binder can be ADA-compliant. Not all natural-looking surfaces are non-compliant. Rubber playground surfaces and poured-in-place rubber are typically compliant. Pavers with tight joints are acceptable.

protruding_objects.title: Protruding Objects
protruding_objects.description: Objects mounted between 27 and 80 inches above floor protrude more than 4 inches into accessible route.

federal_ada.title: Protruding Objects
federal_ada.requirement: Objects mounted on walls with leading edges between 27 and 80 inches above floor must not protrude more than 4 inches horizontally into circulation paths. Objects below 27 inches can protrude any amount (detectable by cane). Objects above 80 inches are unrestricted.

cbc_title24.title: Protruding Objects
cbc_title24.requirement: Same 4-inch maximum protrusion between 27 and 80 inches. CBC additionally requires that protruding objects not reduce the clear width of accessible routes below 36 inches.

protruding_objects.legal_risk: Protruding objects are hazardous to visually impaired persons who cannot detect them with a cane above 27 inches. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Fire extinguisher cabinets, display cases, and signs are common violators.
protruding_objects.cost_note: SF market rate for relocating, recessing, or adding a cane-detectable barrier below a protruding object. Simple barrier addition is cheapest; recessing into wall is most expensive.

remediation.summary: Recess, relocate, or add cane-detectable barriers below protruding objects.
steps: 1. Survey circulation paths for objects protruding more than 4 inches between 27-80 inches height
2. For each violating object, choose solution: recess into wall, relocate, lower below 27 inches, or add cane-detectable barrier
3. For cane-detectable barrier: install permanent element below object within 27 inches of floor
4. Ensure barrier is within the footprint of the protruding object
5. Verify that modifications do not reduce route width below 36 inches
6. No permit typically required for surface-mounted modifications

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Wall-mounted fire extinguisher cabinet protruding into hallway
Shelving or display case jutting out from wall
Wall-mounted TV or monitor at head height
Drinking fountain with front protruding more than 4 inches
Signs or light fixtures on brackets extending from wall
Open stairway underside without cane-detectable barrier
detection.false_positive_notes: Objects below 27 inches are exempt (cane-detectable). Objects above 80 inches are exempt. Objects recessed into alcoves may not protrude into the circulation path. Wall-mounted objects flush with the wall surface are compliant regardless of height.

missing_tactile_signage.title: Missing Tactile Signage (Braille/Raised Characters)
missing_tactile_signage.description: Room or space identification sign lacks required raised characters and Grade 2 Braille.

federal_ada.title: Raised Characters
federal_ada.requirement: Signs identifying permanent rooms and spaces must include raised characters (1/32 inch minimum height) and Grade 2 Braille. Characters must be sans serif, uppercase, and between 5/8 and 2 inches high.

cbc_title24.title: Raised Characters
cbc_title24.requirement: Same requirements as ADA. CBC additionally specifies California Grade 2 Braille and requires that Braille dots be domed or rounded.

missing_tactile_signage.legal_risk: Missing tactile signage denies wayfinding to visually impaired persons. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence per sign. Every room without a tactile sign can be a separate violation. High volume of potential claims in large buildings.
missing_tactile_signage.cost_note: SF market rate for custom ADA-compliant tactile sign with raised characters and Braille. Bulk orders for multiple rooms reduce per-sign cost. Installation labor typically $50-100 per sign.

remediation.summary: Install ADA-compliant tactile signs with raised characters and Braille at all permanent rooms.
steps: 1. Inventory all permanent rooms and spaces requiring identification signs
2. Order ADA-compliant signs with raised characters and Grade 2 Braille
3. Signs must use sans serif, uppercase characters, 5/8 to 2 inches high
4. Mount signs on wall on latch side of door
5. Center of sign at 60 inches above floor (ADA) — note CBC specifies 48-60 inches
6. Provide 18 inches minimum by 18 inches minimum clear floor space centered on sign for tactile reading
7. Signs must have non-glare finish with contrast between characters and background
remediation.contractor_type: Sign company specializing in ADA signage

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction. Low cost per unit makes this a high-value compliance action.

visual_cues: Room identification sign without visible raised text or Braille
Flat-printed signs on doors or walls
Handwritten or paper signs identifying rooms
No sign present at room entrance
Sign with only visual text, no tactile elements
detection.false_positive_notes: Braille and raised characters may not be visible in photos taken from a distance. Directional and informational signs do not require tactile elements — only permanent room/space identification signs do. Temporary signs (event names, personnel names) are exempt.

missing_directional_signage.title: Missing Directional Signage to Accessible Features
missing_directional_signage.description: No directional signs guiding to accessible entrances, restrooms, or other accessible features.

federal_ada.title: Visual Characters
federal_ada.requirement: Signs providing directions to or information about accessible features must include the International Symbol of Accessibility. Must be placed at decision points along accessible routes.

cbc_title24.title: Visual Characters
cbc_title24.requirement: Same ISA requirement on directional signs. CBC additionally requires directional signs at inaccessible entrances pointing to the nearest accessible entrance.

missing_directional_signage.legal_risk: Missing directional signage forces persons with disabilities to search for accessible features. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Often cited alongside the underlying accessibility barrier it should direct to.
missing_directional_signage.cost_note: SF market rate for directional sign with ISA symbol. Includes fabrication and installation. Multiple signs may be needed for complex buildings.

remediation.summary: Install directional signs with ISA symbol at decision points leading to accessible features.
steps: 1. Map all accessible features (entrances, restrooms, elevators, parking)
2. Identify decision points where directional guidance is needed
3. Install directional signs with ISA symbol at each decision point
4. Signs at non-accessible entrances must direct to nearest accessible entrance
5. Use high-contrast, non-glare finish
6. Mount at consistent height visible to both standing and seated persons

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Non-accessible entrance without sign pointing to accessible entrance
Building with multiple entrances but no ISA directional signs
Complex building layout with no wayfinding for accessibility
Accessible restroom exists but no signs directing to it
common_contexts: Buildings with accessible entrance on a different facade
Large commercial buildings with multiple entries
Buildings where accessible route differs from main path
detection.false_positive_notes: If all entrances are accessible, directional signage to an accessible entrance is not required. Small buildings with a single entrance may not need directional signs. Signs may exist inside the building or out of camera view.

missing_detectable_warnings.title: Missing Detectable Warning Surfaces
missing_detectable_warnings.description: No truncated dome surface at curb ramps, transit platforms, or other hazardous vehicular areas.

federal_ada.title: Detectable Warning Surfaces
federal_ada.requirement: Truncated dome detectable warning surfaces must be provided at curb ramps, transit platform edges, and hazardous vehicular areas. Domes must contrast visually with adjacent surfaces (light-on-dark or dark-on-light).

cbc_title24.title: Detectable Warnings
cbc_title24.requirement: Same requirement as ADA. CBC specifies bright yellow as the required color for detectable warnings in California, unlike ADA which only requires visual contrast.

missing_detectable_warnings.legal_risk: Missing detectable warnings endanger visually impaired persons at vehicular boundaries. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Transit-related violations can also involve additional federal claims.
missing_detectable_warnings.cost_note: SF market rate for installing truncated dome detectable warning surface. Adhesive-applied tiles are least expensive; cast-in-place concrete with integral domes costs more but is more durable.

remediation.summary: Install truncated dome detectable warning surfaces at all required locations.
steps: 1. Identify all locations requiring detectable warnings (curb ramps, transit platforms, vehicular crossings)
2. Select detectable warning product (surface-applied tiles, cast-in-place, or retrofit)
3. Prepare surface — clean and level the installation area
4. Install detectable warning surface with bright yellow color (CBC requirement)
5. Ensure visual contrast with adjacent walking surface
6. Truncated domes must be 0.2 inch diameter, 0.9 inch center-to-center spacing
7. Warning surface must extend 24 inches minimum in direction of travel
remediation.contractor_type: Concrete contractor or accessibility product installer

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Curb ramp without raised dome surface
Smooth concrete at curb ramp base
Transit platform edge without tactile strip
Driveway crossing without warning surface
Faded or worn detectable warning with domes missing
detection.false_positive_notes: In California, detectable warnings must be bright yellow. Other colors may indicate non-compliant warnings. Textured concrete that is not truncated domes does not comply. Some older installations use non-standard patterns that may not meet current requirements.

missing_exit_signage.title: Missing Accessible Exit Signage
missing_exit_signage.description: Exit route not identified with accessible exit signs including tactile characters and directional information.

federal_ada.title: Means of Egress
federal_ada.requirement: Signs identifying accessible means of egress must comply with Section 703. Directional signs indicating the accessible means of egress must be provided at exits where not all exits are accessible.

cbc_title24.title: Means of Egress
cbc_title24.requirement: Same requirements as ADA. CBC coordinates with California Fire Code requirements for exit signage, including illuminated exit signs and accessible evacuation route identification.

missing_exit_signage.legal_risk: Missing exit signage endangers persons with disabilities during emergencies. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Fire code violations may compound liability.
missing_exit_signage.cost_note: SF market rate for illuminated accessible exit sign with tactile elements. Includes electrical connection and installation. May require coordination with fire alarm system.

remediation.summary: Install accessible exit signs with tactile elements and directional information at all required locations.
steps: 1. Review means of egress plan with fire safety consultant
2. Identify all exit locations and which are accessible
3. Install tactile exit signs at accessible exits
4. Install directional signs at non-accessible exits pointing to accessible exits
5. Ensure illuminated exit signs meet California Fire Code requirements
6. Coordinate with fire alarm and emergency lighting systems
7. Apply for SF DBI electrical permit if new electrical connections needed

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Exit door without illuminated exit sign
Exit sign without tactile characters or Braille
No directional exit signage in corridors
Accessible exit not differentiated from non-accessible exits
common_contexts: Older buildings without updated exit signage
detection.false_positive_notes: Standard illuminated EXIT signs may comply with fire code but not ADA tactile requirements. The issue is often missing Braille/raised characters on exit identification signs, not the illuminated sign itself.

missing_isa_symbol.title: Missing International Symbol of Accessibility
missing_isa_symbol.description: Required ISA symbol not displayed at accessible features or on required signs.

federal_ada.title: Symbols of Accessibility
federal_ada.requirement: The International Symbol of Accessibility must be displayed at accessible entrances (when not all are accessible), accessible parking spaces, accessible restrooms, and other accessible features that are not universally provided.

cbc_title24.title: Symbols of Accessibility
cbc_title24.requirement: Same ISA display requirements. CBC requires the ISA symbol to be white on a blue background for maximum visibility and standardization.

missing_isa_symbol.legal_risk: Missing ISA symbols make it difficult for persons with disabilities to identify accessible features. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Low severity but easy to document and claim.
missing_isa_symbol.cost_note: SF market rate for ISA symbol sign or decal. Very low cost item — primarily labor for proper placement.

remediation.summary: Install ISA symbol signs at all required accessible features.
steps: 1. Identify all locations requiring ISA symbol (accessible entrances, parking, restrooms, elevators)
2. Purchase ISA symbol signs or decals (white on blue background)
3. Install at designated locations per ADA mounting requirements
4. Ensure visibility from primary approach direction

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction. Minimal cost — among the easiest compliance wins.

visual_cues: Accessible entrance without wheelchair symbol
Accessible restroom without ISA sign
Accessible parking without ISA on sign
Elevator without ISA identification
common_contexts: Buildings with mixed accessible and non-accessible entrances
Buildings with accessible features on different floors
detection.false_positive_notes: ISA symbol is not required if all features of that type are accessible (e.g., if all entrances are accessible, ISA is not required at entrances). The symbol may be present on another side of the sign or door not visible in the photo.

non_compliant_sign_mounting.title: Non-Compliant Sign Mounting Height/Location
non_compliant_sign_mounting.description: Tactile sign mounted at wrong height or location relative to the door it identifies.

federal_ada.title: Installation Height and Location
federal_ada.requirement: Tactile signs identifying rooms must be mounted on the wall adjacent to the latch side of the door, with the baseline of the lowest tactile character at 48 inches minimum and the baseline of the highest character at 60 inches maximum above the floor.

cbc_title24.title: Installation Height and Location
cbc_title24.requirement: CBC specifies signs shall be located so the centerline is at 60 inches above the finished floor. Sign must be on the latch side of the door with 18 x 18 inch clear floor space for reading.

non_compliant_sign_mounting.legal_risk: Incorrectly mounted signs cannot be found by visually impaired persons who rely on consistent placement conventions. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Often included as part of a bundle of signage violations.
non_compliant_sign_mounting.cost_note: SF market rate for relocating an existing sign to the correct position. May require patching holes from previous mounting location.

remediation.summary: Relocate sign to correct height and position on latch side of door.
steps: 1. Identify which door side is the latch side (where the handle/lock is)
2. Remove sign from current location
3. Patch any holes from previous mounting
4. Remount sign on wall on latch side of door
5. Position so centerline is at 60 inches above finished floor (CBC standard)
6. Ensure 18 x 18 inch clear floor space centered on sign for tactile reading
7. If double doors with no wall space, mount on nearest adjacent wall

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Tactile sign mounted on door instead of adjacent wall
Sign mounted too high or too low on wall
Sign mounted on hinge side of door instead of latch side
Sign centered on door frame rather than offset to latch side
common_contexts: Signs on wrong side of door
