detection.false_positive_notes: Directional and informational signs do not have the same mounting requirements as room identification signs. Only permanent room/space identification signs with tactile elements must be at specific heights on the latch side.

inaccessible_restroom.title: Generally Inaccessible Restroom
inaccessible_restroom.description: Restroom fails to meet multiple accessibility requirements making it unusable for persons with disabilities.

federal_ada.title: Toilet and Bathing Rooms
federal_ada.requirement: At least one of each type of toilet room provided must be accessible. Must include 60-inch turning space, accessible fixtures, proper signage, and be on an accessible route. All components must comply with respective standards.

cbc_title24.title: Toilet and Bathing Rooms
cbc_title24.requirement: Same requirements as ADA. CBC additionally requires that accessible restrooms have visual contrast on door hardware and that emergency call systems be provided in single-user accessible restrooms.

inaccessible_restroom.legal_risk: Inaccessible restrooms are among the most commonly litigated ADA violations. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence per visit. Restroom violations often involve multiple sub-violations (grab bars, clearances, fixtures), each potentially a separate claim.
inaccessible_restroom.cost_note: SF market rate for full restroom renovation to ADA compliance. Includes plumbing, fixture replacement, grab bars, door modifications, and finish work. Minor fixes (grab bars only) may cost as little as $1,000.

remediation.summary: Renovate restroom to meet all ADA accessibility requirements.
steps: 1. Conduct CASp inspection to identify all non-compliant elements
2. Develop remediation plan addressing: turning space, stall size, grab bars, fixture heights, door width, hardware
3. Apply for SF DBI plumbing and building permits
4. Widen doorway to 32-inch minimum clear width if needed
5. Replace round knobs with lever handles
6. Provide 60-inch turning space (may require stall reconfiguration)
7. Install grab bars per ADA 604.5
8. Adjust or replace toilet, sink, mirror, and dispensers to compliant heights
9. Install tactile signage with ISA symbol
10. Schedule final inspection
remediation.permit_type: SF DBI Building and Plumbing Permits

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction. Large projects may benefit from combining both credits.

visual_cues: Narrow restroom door
No grab bars visible
Small restroom with no turning space
Non-compliant fixtures visible through open door
No ISA signage on restroom
Round door knob on restroom door
common_contexts: Retail stores with original construction restrooms
detection.false_positive_notes: A restroom may appear small but still meet minimum requirements. Single-user restrooms have different requirements than multi-stall. An accessible stall in a larger restroom may satisfy requirements even if the entire restroom doesn't appear accessible.

missing_grab_bars.title: Missing Grab Bars
missing_grab_bars.description: Accessible toilet compartment lacks required grab bars on side wall and rear wall.

federal_ada.title: Grab Bars
federal_ada.requirement: Grab bars must be provided on the side wall and rear wall closest to the toilet. Side grab bar: 42 inches minimum, starting 12 inches from rear wall. Rear grab bar: 36 inches minimum. Mounted 33-36 inches above floor.

cbc_title24.title: Grab Bars
cbc_title24.requirement: Same grab bar specifications as ADA. CBC additionally requires that grab bars have a 1-1/4 to 2-inch diameter and be able to support 250 lbs of force.

missing_grab_bars.legal_risk: Missing grab bars prevent safe toilet transfers for wheelchair users. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Grab bars are among the most commonly cited restroom violations and are inexpensive to fix, making non-compliance particularly indefensible.
missing_grab_bars.cost_note: SF market rate for installing both side and rear grab bars in one restroom stall. Includes stainless steel bars, wall reinforcement if needed (backing plates or blocking), and labor.

remediation.summary: Install compliant grab bars on side wall and rear wall of accessible toilet compartment.
steps: 1. Verify wall can support 250 lbs of force (add wood blocking behind drywall if needed)
2. Install side wall grab bar: 42 inches minimum length, 12 inches max from rear wall, extending 54 inches from rear wall
3. Install rear wall grab bar: 36 inches minimum length, centered on toilet or offset per ADA requirements
4. Mount both bars at 33-36 inches above finished floor
5. Use 1-1/4 to 2-inch diameter stainless steel grab bars
6. Maintain 1-1/2 inch clearance between bar and wall
7. Test load capacity (250 lbs minimum)
remediation.contractor_type: General contractor or handyman (may need carpenter for wall backing)

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction. Very cost-effective compliance fix.

visual_cues: Toilet without grab bars on adjacent walls
Towel bars used in place of proper grab bars (not rated for 250 lbs)
Grab bar on only one wall (missing second bar)
Grab bar mounted at incorrect height or length
common_contexts: Recently renovated restrooms that overlooked accessibility
detection.false_positive_notes: Decorative towel bars are not grab bars and do not meet strength requirements. Fold-down grab bars are acceptable if they meet length and strength requirements. Grab bars may exist but be partially hidden by the toilet or partition in photos.

insufficient_stall_size.title: Insufficient Accessible Stall Size
insufficient_stall_size.description: Wheelchair-accessible toilet stall does not meet minimum width and depth requirements.

federal_ada.title: Toilet Compartments
federal_ada.requirement: Wheelchair-accessible compartments must be 60 inches wide minimum. Depth: 56 inches minimum for wall-hung toilets, 59 inches minimum for floor-mounted toilets. Door must provide 32-inch clear width and swing out.

cbc_title24.title: Toilet Compartments
cbc_title24.requirement: Same minimum dimensions as ADA. CBC additionally requires that the door be self-closing and that the stall have a coat hook at 48 inches maximum height.

insufficient_stall_size.legal_risk: Undersized stalls prevent wheelchair users from entering, transferring, and using the toilet. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Stall size violations typically require significant renovation to fix.
insufficient_stall_size.cost_note: SF market rate for reconfiguring toilet stall partitions and potentially relocating fixtures to achieve compliant dimensions. May require reducing number of stalls.

remediation.summary: Reconfigure accessible stall to meet minimum 60-inch width and required depth.
steps: 1. Measure existing stall dimensions and identify deficiencies
2. Design reconfiguration plan — may involve combining two stalls or removing adjacent fixtures
3. Apply for SF DBI permit if plumbing relocation needed
4. Relocate partition walls to achieve 60-inch minimum width
5. Ensure depth meets requirements (56 or 59 inches depending on toilet type)
6. Install outward-swinging door with 32-inch minimum clear width
7. Install grab bars, coat hook, and other required accessories
8. Verify 60-inch turning space within stall or room

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Accessible stall that appears too narrow for wheelchair
Stall door that swings inward (reducing usable space)
Toilet very close to side partition
No visible turning space within stall
common_contexts: Older restrooms with original partition layouts
Multi-stall restrooms where accessible stall was not properly designed
detection.false_positive_notes: Ambulatory accessible stalls (36 inches wide) are a different stall type than wheelchair accessible and have different size requirements. A stall may appear small in a photo but meet requirements. Floor-mounted vs. wall-hung toilets have different depth requirements.

high_mirror.title: Mirror Mounted Too High
high_mirror.description: Restroom mirror bottom edge is higher than 40 inches above the floor, inaccessible to seated users.

federal_ada.title: Mirrors
federal_ada.requirement: Mirrors above lavatories must have the bottom edge of the reflecting surface 40 inches maximum above the finished floor. Full-length mirrors may be mounted at any height.

cbc_title24.title: Mirrors
cbc_title24.requirement: Same 40-inch maximum height requirement. CBC specifies that mirrors must be mounted with the bottom edge of the reflecting surface at 40 inches maximum, not the bottom edge of the frame.

high_mirror.legal_risk: High-mounted mirrors deny equal access to seated persons. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Easy to document and fix, making non-compliance hard to defend.
high_mirror.cost_note: SF market rate for lowering or replacing a restroom mirror. Includes mirror, mounting hardware, and wall repair from previous mounting holes.

remediation.summary: Lower mirror so bottom edge of reflecting surface is at 40 inches or less above floor.
steps: 1. Measure current height of mirror's bottom reflecting edge above floor
2. If too high, remove mirror and lower mounting position
3. Alternatively, install a tilted mirror or full-length mirror
4. Patch and repaint wall from previous mounting holes
5. Verify bottom of reflecting surface (not frame) is at 40 inches maximum

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction. Very low cost.

visual_cues: Mirror mounted high above sink with significant gap between counter and mirror bottom
Mirror bottom edge visually above waist height of standing person
Mirror that a seated person clearly could not use
Mirror mounted at or near eye level for standing person only
common_contexts: Commercial restrooms with mirrors above sinks
Restrooms in restaurants, offices, and retail
detection.false_positive_notes: The measurement is to the bottom of the reflecting surface, not the frame. Tilted mirrors may have their bottom edge above 40 inches but still comply because the reflecting surface is angled to be usable. Full-length mirrors at any height are compliant.

insufficient_sink_clearance.title: Insufficient Sink Knee Clearance
insufficient_sink_clearance.description: Sink does not provide adequate knee and toe clearance for wheelchair approach.

federal_ada.title: Clear Floor Space
federal_ada.requirement: A clear floor space complying with Section 305, positioned for forward approach, must be provided at sinks. Knee clearance: 27 inches minimum height, 8 inches minimum depth at 27 inches, 11 inches minimum depth at 9 inches above floor. Sink rim maximum 34 inches above floor.

cbc_title24.title: Clear Floor Space
cbc_title24.requirement: Same clearance requirements as ADA. CBC additionally requires insulation or covering of exposed water supply and drain pipes to prevent contact burns and abrasion injuries.

insufficient_sink_clearance.legal_risk: Insufficient sink clearance prevents wheelchair users from washing hands independently. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Exposed pipes creating burn risks add premises liability exposure.
insufficient_sink_clearance.cost_note: SF market rate for replacing or modifying a sink for wheelchair clearance. Includes wall-mount sink, plumbing modifications, pipe insulation, and finish work.

remediation.summary: Modify or replace sink to provide required knee and toe clearance with pipe protection.
steps: 1. Measure existing knee clearance under sink
2. If vanity/cabinet: remove cabinet and install wall-mounted sink at 34 inches maximum height
3. Ensure 27 inches minimum knee clearance height
4. Install pipe insulation on exposed supply and drain pipes (CBC requirement)
5. Provide clear floor space for forward wheelchair approach
6. Install lever-operated or sensor faucet
7. Apply for SF DBI plumbing permit if significant modifications needed

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Sink with vanity cabinet underneath blocking knee space
Pedestal sink with wide base blocking approach
Sink mounted too high for seated use
Exposed hot water pipes without insulation under sink
detection.false_positive_notes: Wall-mounted sinks at proper height typically provide adequate clearance. The presence of a cabinet does not automatically mean non-compliance — some accessible vanities provide required clearance. An apron-front sink may look like it blocks clearance but could comply.

non_compliant_toilet_height.title: Non-Compliant Toilet Seat Height
non_compliant_toilet_height.description: Toilet seat height is not within the required 17-19 inch range measured from floor to top of seat.

federal_ada.title: Seat Height
federal_ada.requirement: The top of the toilet seat must be 17 inches minimum and 19 inches maximum above the finished floor, measured to the top of the seat.

cbc_title24.title: Seat Height
cbc_title24.requirement: Same 17-19 inch seat height requirement. CBC specifies measurement to top of seat in the down position.

non_compliant_toilet_height.legal_risk: Non-standard toilet height prevents safe transfers from wheelchair. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Often cited with other restroom violations in serial litigation.
non_compliant_toilet_height.cost_note: SF market rate for replacing a toilet with a compliant-height model (comfort height/ADA toilet). Includes fixture, installation, and wax ring. Wall-hung toilet adjustment may cost more.

remediation.summary: Replace toilet with ADA-compliant height model (17-19 inches to top of seat).
steps: 1. Measure existing toilet seat height from floor to top of seat
2. If too low: install toilet riser or replace with comfort-height (ADA) toilet
3. If too high: replace with standard ADA-compliant toilet
4. Connect water supply and drain
5. Install new wax ring and verify no leaks
6. Measure final seat height to confirm 17-19 inch range
7. No permit typically required for fixture replacement on existing plumbing

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Toilet that appears noticeably low (standard residential height ~15 inches)
Toilet with added riser indicating original was too low
Toilet seat visibly below or above wheelchair seat level (~18 inches)
common_contexts: Older restrooms with original residential-height toilets
Recently renovated restrooms where contractor used standard (non-ADA) toilets
detection.false_positive_notes: Cannot reliably determine toilet height from photos without a reference measurement. 'Comfort height' toilets marketed for residential use are typically 17-19 inches and comply. The measurement includes the seat, not just the bowl rim.

inaccessible_flush_control.title: Inaccessible Flush Control Placement
inaccessible_flush_control.description: Toilet flush control is mounted on the wrong side, requiring reach across the toilet.

federal_ada.title: Flush Controls
federal_ada.requirement: Flush controls must be hand-operated or automatic. Hand-operated controls must be on the open (transfer) side of the toilet and mounted 44 inches maximum above the floor. Must not require tight grasping, pinching, or twisting.

cbc_title24.title: Flush Controls
cbc_title24.requirement: Same requirements as ADA. CBC allows automatic sensor-activated flush as an alternative to hand-operated controls on the open side.

inaccessible_flush_control.legal_risk: Flush controls on the wall side require unsafe reaching across the toilet. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Often cited as part of a bundle of restroom violations.
inaccessible_flush_control.cost_note: SF market rate for installing an automatic flush sensor (most cost-effective fix) or relocating flush handle to open side. Sensor installation avoids plumbing modifications.

remediation.summary: Relocate flush control to open side of toilet or install automatic flush sensor.
steps: 1. Identify the open (transfer) side of the toilet based on stall layout
2. Option A: Install automatic flush sensor (preferred — eliminates hand-operation requirement entirely)
3. Option B: Replace flush valve with one having handle on open side
4. If handle relocation requires plumbing changes, apply for SF DBI plumbing permit
5. Verify control operates without tight grasping, pinching, or twisting
6. Ensure control is 44 inches maximum above floor

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Flush handle on wall side of toilet (side closest to wall, not open stall area)
Flush control requiring reach across or behind toilet
No automatic flush sensor visible
common_contexts: Standard toilet installations where plumber did not consider ADA transfer side
Restrooms where stall layout was changed but toilet orientation wasn't
detection.false_positive_notes: The open side depends on the stall layout, which may not be fully visible in a photo. Left-hand and right-hand transfer configurations affect which side is the open side. Automatic flush sensors make handle placement moot.

inaccessible_dispenser_placement.title: Inaccessible Dispenser Placement
inaccessible_dispenser_placement.description: Toilet paper, soap, or paper towel dispensers mounted too high or out of reach range.

federal_ada.title: Dispensers
federal_ada.requirement: Dispensers for toilet paper, soap, paper towels, and other items must be within reach range (15-48 inches above floor for forward reach, 15-46 inches for side reach). Must be operable with one hand without tight grasping.

cbc_title24.title: Dispensers
cbc_title24.requirement: Same reach range requirements as ADA. CBC requires that toilet paper dispensers be mounted 19 inches minimum above floor and 36 inches maximum from the rear wall. Dispensers shall not be behind grab bars.

inaccessible_dispenser_placement.legal_risk: Inaccessible dispensers prevent independent restroom use. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence per dispenser. Multiple dispensers in one restroom can mean multiple violations.
inaccessible_dispenser_placement.cost_note: SF market rate for relocating a dispenser to compliant height and reach range. Includes patching from old mounting holes and new installation.

remediation.summary: Relocate dispensers to within reach range and proper position.
steps: 1. Measure current height and position of all dispensers
2. Relocate toilet paper dispenser: 19 inches minimum above floor, 36 inches maximum from rear wall, not behind grab bar
3. Relocate soap dispensers: 44 inches maximum above floor for forward reach
4. Relocate paper towel/dryer: 44 inches maximum above floor for forward approach
5. Patch wall from previous mounting locations
6. Verify all dispensers operable with one hand

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Paper towel dispenser mounted at head height or higher
Soap dispenser above counter at high position
Toilet paper dispenser behind grab bar or far from toilet
Dispenser requiring significant reaching from seated position
common_contexts: Restrooms where dispensers were placed for standing users
Recently renovated restrooms with improperly placed dispensers
detection.false_positive_notes: Dispensers at 44-48 inches may appear high but could be within compliant reach range depending on approach direction. Counter-mounted soap dispensers may comply if the counter is within reach range.

inaccessible_counter.title: Inaccessible Service Counter
inaccessible_counter.description: Sales or service counter exceeds 36-inch maximum height with no accessible section or alternative.

federal_ada.title: Sales and Service Counters
federal_ada.requirement: A portion of the counter surface must be 36 inches maximum above the floor and 36 inches minimum in length. Alternatively, an auxiliary counter or equivalent facilitation must be provided.

cbc_title24.title: Sales and Service Counters
cbc_title24.requirement: CBC requires 34-inch maximum counter height (stricter than ADA's 36 inches) for at least one accessible service position. Must be at least 36 inches long.

inaccessible_counter.legal_risk: Inaccessible counters prevent wheelchair users from conducting transactions. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Retail and food service businesses are heavily targeted for counter height violations in California.
inaccessible_counter.cost_note: SF market rate for lowering a 36-inch section of counter or adding an auxiliary counter. Includes demolition, new counter construction, and finish work. Simple auxiliary counter addition may cost less.

remediation.summary: Lower a section of counter to 34 inches (CBC) or provide auxiliary counter at accessible height.
steps: 1. Measure current counter height
2. Option A: Lower a 36-inch minimum section of existing counter to 34 inches maximum (CBC standard)
3. Option B: Add an auxiliary counter or writing surface at 34 inches maximum height
4. Option C: Provide equivalent facilitation (clipboard, adjacent table)
5. Ensure counter depth at accessible section matches rest of counter
6. Provide knee clearance for forward approach if applicable
7. No permit typically required for counter modifications

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Service counter at chest height for standing person
Counter that a seated person clearly cannot see over or reach across
No visible lowered section of counter
Cash register or POS terminal on high counter with no accessible alternative
detection.false_positive_notes: A counter that appears high may have a lowered section out of frame. Some businesses use a separate accessible service area. A clipboard provided for transactions at a seated level can constitute equivalent facilitation.

inaccessible_atm_or_kiosk.title: Inaccessible ATM or Kiosk
inaccessible_atm_or_kiosk.description: Self-service kiosk, ATM, or interactive terminal is not accessible to persons with disabilities.

federal_ada.title: Automatic Teller Machines and Fare Machines
federal_ada.requirement: ATMs and fare machines must provide accessible operable parts within reach range (15-48 inches), speech output capability, Braille instructions, and input controls usable without vision. Clear floor space for forward or parallel approach.

cbc_title24.title: Automatic Teller Machines and Fare Machines
cbc_title24.requirement: Same requirements as ADA. CBC additionally requires that at least one ATM at each location be accessible and on an accessible route.

inaccessible_atm_or_kiosk.legal_risk: Inaccessible kiosks exclude persons with mobility and vision disabilities from self-service. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. ATM and kiosk accessibility has become an active litigation area in California.
inaccessible_atm_or_kiosk.cost_note: SF market rate for replacing or modifying a kiosk for accessibility. Software modifications for speech output may be additional. Some kiosk manufacturers offer ADA-compliant models as replacements.

remediation.summary: Replace or modify kiosk to provide accessible controls, reach range, and speech output.
steps: 1. Evaluate current kiosk for accessibility deficiencies
2. Check reach range: all operable parts must be 15-48 inches above floor
3. Ensure clear floor space for wheelchair approach (30 x 48 inches minimum)
4. Verify or add speech output capability with privacy option (headphone jack)
5. Add Braille instructions and tactile controls
6. If modifications not feasible, replace with ADA-compliant model
7. Ensure kiosk is on an accessible route
remediation.timeline: 2-6 weeks depending on replacement vs. modification

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Kiosk with touchscreen mounted above comfortable seated reach
ATM on raised platform with no ramp
Self-checkout terminal with no clear floor space for wheelchair
Payment terminal mounted too high on pole or stand
common_contexts: Check-in kiosks at hotels and airports
detection.false_positive_notes: Many modern ATMs are designed to be accessible. A kiosk may appear too high but actually have operable parts within reach range. Check if an alternative accessible kiosk exists at the same location.

inaccessible_queue.title: Inaccessible Queue or Waiting Line
inaccessible_queue.description: Queue or waiting line configuration does not allow wheelchair passage or circulation.

federal_ada.title: Walking Surfaces - Clear Width
federal_ada.requirement: Queues and waiting lines must provide a minimum 36-inch clear width for wheelchair passage. Turns within queue must provide adequate clearance for wheelchair turning.

cbc_title24.title: Clear Width
cbc_title24.requirement: Same 36-inch minimum width requirement. CBC does not allow the ADA's 32-inch constriction exception, making all queue sections require 36 inches minimum.

inaccessible_queue.legal_risk: Inaccessible queues force wheelchair users to bypass lines, creating unequal service. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Stanchion-based queues that are too narrow or have tight switchbacks are common violators.
inaccessible_queue.cost_note: SF market rate for reconfiguring stanchion-based queue layout. If permanent fixtures (railings, counters) define the queue, cost may be significantly higher.

remediation.summary: Reconfigure queue layout to provide minimum 36-inch clear width throughout with accessible turns.
steps: 1. Measure clear width of queue at all points including turns
2. Reconfigure stanchions or barriers to provide 36-inch minimum clear width
3. At 180-degree turns, provide 60-inch turning space or 48-inch minimum aisle width
4. Remove any steps or level changes within the queue path
5. Train staff on maintaining accessible queue configuration
6. Establish bypass procedure for wheelchair users if queue cannot be fully modified
remediation.contractor_type: Facility management or general contractor for permanent fixtures
remediation.timeline: 1-3 days for stanchion-based; 2-4 weeks for permanent fixture modifications

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Narrow stanchion queue lanes
Queue with tight switchback turns
Rope barriers creating paths too narrow for wheelchair
Queue involving steps or level changes
common_contexts: Box office and ticket counter areas
detection.false_positive_notes: Stanchion-based queues can be easily reconfigured — the current photo may show a non-accessible setup that could be different at other times. Staff may provide queue bypass for wheelchair users (though this is not a substitute for an accessible queue).

inaccessible_dining_surface.title: Inaccessible Dining Surface
inaccessible_dining_surface.description: Dining or work surface does not provide wheelchair clearance or is at non-compliant height.

federal_ada.title: Dining Surfaces and Work Surfaces
federal_ada.requirement: Dining and work surface tops must be 28-34 inches above floor. Knee clearance: 27 inches minimum height, 30 inches minimum width, and 19 inches minimum depth. At least 5% of seating (but not less than 1) must be accessible.

cbc_title24.title: Dining Surfaces and Work Surfaces
cbc_title24.requirement: Same height and clearance requirements as ADA. CBC requires that accessible seating be distributed throughout the dining area, not clustered in one location.

inaccessible_dining_surface.legal_risk: Inaccessible dining surfaces prevent wheelchair users from eating at restaurants or using work tables. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Restaurant dining accessibility is actively litigated in San Francisco.
inaccessible_dining_surface.cost_note: SF market rate for providing accessible dining surfaces. May involve replacing fixed booth seating with accessible tables, modifying existing tables for knee clearance, or adding wheelchair-accessible positions.

remediation.summary: Provide accessible dining surfaces with proper height and knee clearance at 5% of seating positions.
steps: 1. Count total seating positions and calculate 5% requirement (minimum 1)
2. Identify or create accessible dining positions with 28-34 inch table height
3. Ensure 27-inch knee clearance height, 30-inch width, 19-inch depth
4. Remove fixed chairs from accessible positions to allow wheelchair approach
5. Distribute accessible seating throughout dining area (not all in one section)
6. Ensure accessible seating is on an accessible route
7. Maintain accessible positions during service (don't block with chairs or equipment)
remediation.contractor_type: Restaurant furniture supplier or general contractor for built-in seating

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: All dining seating is fixed booths with no wheelchair-accessible tables
Tables with center pedestals blocking knee clearance
High bar-height tables only
Closely spaced tables without wheelchair circulation room
detection.false_positive_notes: Standard table height (29-30 inches) typically complies with the 28-34 inch requirement. Movable chairs can usually be removed to create wheelchair space. At least one accessible table is often present even if not obvious in photos.

path_of_travel_trigger.title: Path of Travel Accessibility Trigger
path_of_travel_trigger.description: Building renovation exceeds California's 20% valuation threshold, triggering mandatory accessibility upgrades beyond the area of alteration.

federal_ada.title: Alterations: Path of Travel
federal_ada.requirement: When a primary function area is altered, the path of travel to the altered area must be made accessible. Obligation limited to 20% of the overall cost of the alteration (disproportionate cost defense).

cbc_title24.title: Path of Travel Requirements
cbc_title24.requirement: When alterations, structural repairs, or additions are made, an accessible path of travel to the altered area is required. Cost limited to 20% of adjusted construction cost. For projects exceeding the valuation threshold ($209,208 in 2026), full compliance may be required unless unreasonable hardship is demonstrated.

sf_local.title: Accessibility Upgrades for Alterations
sf_local.requirement: SF DBI enforces CBC path of travel requirements. Projects exceeding the valuation threshold must demonstrate unreasonable hardship to limit path of travel compliance. All TI projects require accessibility review.

path_of_travel_trigger.legal_risk: Failure to address path of travel during renovations violates CBC and creates ongoing Unruh Act exposure ($4,000+ per occurrence). SF DBI may withhold permits or certificate of occupancy for non-compliance.
path_of_travel_trigger.cost_note: Path of travel upgrades typically cost 10-20% of the renovation budget. For a $200,000 renovation, expect $20,000-$40,000 in accessibility upgrades covering entrance, route, restrooms, drinking fountains, and signage.

remediation.summary: Evaluate and implement required accessibility upgrades along path of travel as part of renovation project.
steps: 1. Calculate adjusted construction cost of renovation project
2. Compare against current valuation threshold ($209,208 for 2026)
3. Determine scope of path of travel elements: primary entrance, route to altered area, restrooms, drinking fountains, signage
4. If under threshold: allocate up to 20% of construction cost to accessibility upgrades
5. If over threshold: plan for full accessibility compliance unless unreasonable hardship demonstrated
6. Prioritize upgrades per CBC priority order: entrance access, route access, restroom access, other elements
7. Include accessibility scope in SF DBI permit application
8. Engage CASp inspector for compliance verification
remediation.contractor_type: Licensed general contractor with CASp consultant
remediation.permit_type: SF DBI Building Permit (included in renovation permit)

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction. Path of travel expenses are part of the renovation cost basis.

visual_cues: Active renovation or construction at building
Construction permits visible in window
Recently completed renovation with no accessibility improvements visible
Modernized interior with outdated or inaccessible entrance
detection.false_positive_notes: Not all renovations trigger path of travel requirements — only alterations to primary function areas. Cosmetic changes (paint, carpet, fixtures) may not trigger requirements. The 20% cost limitation provides some protection for small renovations.

missing_casp_inspection.title: Missing CASp Inspection
missing_casp_inspection.description: Business has not obtained a Certified Access Specialist (CASp) inspection as recommended or required by California law.

cbc_title24.title: Certified Access Specialist Program
cbc_title24.requirement: California SB 1608 created the CASp program. A CASp inspection provides qualified defendant status in construction-related accessibility claims, requiring early court evaluation of compliance and 90-day stay for correction.

sf_local.title: CASp Requirement
sf_local.requirement: SF Accessible Business Entrance program requires CASp inspection or licensed architect/engineer review for certain building categories. CASp reports are filed with SF DBI.

missing_casp_inspection.legal_risk: Without a CASp inspection, businesses lose significant legal protections in California. CASp-inspected businesses get: 90-day stay to fix violations before lawsuit proceeds, early evaluation of claims, and reduced statutory damages ($1,000-$2,000 instead of $4,000+). The cost of inspection is tiny compared to litigation exposure.
missing_casp_inspection.cost_note: SF market rate for a comprehensive CASp inspection of a commercial property. Small retail spaces may cost less ($1,500-$2,500). Large facilities with parking and multiple floors can exceed $5,000.

remediation.summary: Hire a Certified Access Specialist to conduct a full accessibility inspection and provide a CASp report.
steps: 1. Find a Certified Access Specialist through the California Division of the State Architect (DSA) directory
2. Schedule comprehensive accessibility inspection of the property
3. CASp will inspect: entrances, routes, parking, restrooms, counters, signage, and all public-facing elements
4. Receive CASp inspection report with findings and remediation recommendations
5. Post CASp inspection certificate in a visible location (provides legal notice)
6. Develop remediation plan based on CASp findings with prioritization
7. File required documentation with SF DBI for ABE program compliance
8. Implement corrections and schedule follow-up CASp verification if needed

tax_credits.notes: CASp inspection costs are eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit (small businesses) and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction. The inspection itself is a deductible business expense.

visual_cues: No CASp inspection certificate posted in window or entry
Multiple visible accessibility violations suggesting no professional review
Building with no accessibility improvements despite being in business
common_contexts: Small businesses unaware of CASp program
New businesses that did not conduct pre-opening accessibility review
Businesses in older buildings with no recent renovations
detection.false_positive_notes: A CASp inspection may have been completed but the certificate is not visible in the photo. Some businesses display certificates inside rather than in the window. The absence of visible violations does not mean a CASp inspection was conducted, and vice versa.

sf_business_entrance_noncompliant.title: SF Accessible Business Entrance Non-Compliance
sf_business_entrance_noncompliant.description: Business entrance fails to meet San Francisco's Accessible Business Entrance (ABE) program requirements.

federal_ada.title: Entrances
federal_ada.requirement: Federal ADA requires at least 60% of public entrances to be accessible. SF ABE program goes further by requiring all primary business entrances to be accessible.

cbc_title24.title: Entrances
cbc_title24.requirement: CBC requires accessible entrances with the primary entrance being accessible when technically feasible.

sf_local.title: Accessible Business Entrance Program
sf_local.requirement: All primary entrances to places of public accommodation must be accessible. Property owners must: obtain inspection, submit compliance checklist, apply for permits within 90 days of checklist submission, and complete improvements by category deadline.

sf_business_entrance_noncompliant.legal_risk: Non-compliance with SF ABE program results in enforcement action by SF DBI in addition to Unruh Act litigation exposure ($4,000+ per occurrence). SF DBI may issue Notice of Violation and daily penalties for missed deadlines.
sf_business_entrance_noncompliant.cost_note: SF market rate for bringing a business entrance into ABE compliance, including ramp or threshold modifications, door widening, hardware replacement, and signage. Costs vary widely based on existing conditions.

remediation.summary: Bring primary business entrance into full compliance with SF ABE program requirements.
steps: 1. Determine building's ABE category (1-4 based on building type and occupancy)
2. Hire CASp or licensed architect/engineer to complete Category Checklist Compliance Form
3. Submit completed checklist to SF DBI
4. Apply for necessary building permits within 90 days of checklist submission
5. Complete entrance accessibility improvements (ramp, door, threshold, hardware, signage)
6. Schedule SF DBI inspection for permit sign-off
7. Maintain compliance documentation
remediation.contractor_type: Licensed general contractor with CASp consultant
remediation.permit_type: SF DBI OTC Permit (Accessible Business Entrance compliance)

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Steps at primary business entrance with no ramp
Narrow door at main entrance
Non-compliant threshold at primary entrance
No ABE compliance documentation visible
Business in older building with no entrance accessibility improvements
common_contexts: Storefronts in San Francisco's older commercial districts
Businesses in Victorian or Edwardian-era buildings
Restaurants and retail in hilly neighborhoods
detection.false_positive_notes: Some buildings may have received hardship exemptions from SF DBI. Technical infeasibility determinations may allow alternative compliance measures. A building may be in an approved compliance timeline with work pending.

historic_building_no_alt_compliance.title: Historic Building Without Alternative Compliance Plan
historic_building_no_alt_compliance.description: Qualified historic building has not established an alternative compliance plan for accessibility.

federal_ada.title: Alterations to Qualified Historic Buildings
federal_ada.requirement: Alterations to qualified historic buildings must comply with 2010 ADA Standards to the maximum extent feasible. When full compliance would threaten or destroy historical significance, alternative methods of access may be used.

cbc_title24.title: Qualified Historical Buildings and Facilities
cbc_title24.requirement: CBC allows alternative compliance methods for qualified historical buildings when standard compliance would threaten the historical significance. Must use alternative compliance per California Historical Building Code (CHBC). State Historic Preservation Officer consultation may be required.

sf_local.title: Historic Preservation
sf_local.requirement: SF landmarks and buildings in historic districts may qualify for alternative compliance. Must coordinate with SF Planning Department Historic Preservation Commission and SF DBI.

historic_building_no_alt_compliance.legal_risk: Historic buildings are not exempt from accessibility requirements. Without an alternative compliance plan, the building must meet standard ADA/CBC requirements. Under the Unruh Act, $4,000+ per occurrence. Historic status is not a defense without a documented alternative compliance plan.
historic_building_no_alt_compliance.cost_note: SF market rate for developing an alternative compliance plan for a historic building. Includes CASp inspection, historical architect consultation, and documentation. Actual remediation costs are additional.

remediation.summary: Develop and document an alternative compliance plan that provides maximum accessibility while preserving historic character.
steps: 1. Verify building's qualification as a historic structure (National Register, California Register, or local landmark)
2. Hire architect experienced in both historic preservation and accessibility
3. Conduct CASp inspection identifying all accessibility barriers
4. Evaluate which standard compliance measures would threaten historical significance
5. Develop alternative compliance methods per California Historical Building Code (CHBC)
6. Document rationale for each alternative method
7. Submit alternative compliance plan to SF DBI and SF Historic Preservation Commission if applicable
8. Implement approved alternative methods
9. Maintain plan documentation on file
remediation.contractor_type: Historic preservation architect with CASp consultant
remediation.permit_type: SF DBI Building Permit with Historic Preservation review

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction. Historic Rehabilitation Tax Credits (federal and state) may also apply if building is income-producing.

visual_cues: Clearly historic building (Victorian, Edwardian, Art Deco architecture) with no accessibility features
Historic plaque or designation visible with no accessible entrance
Ornate entrance with steps and no ramp or alternative access
common_contexts: San Francisco Victorian and Edwardian buildings
Buildings in historic districts (Jackson Square, Civic Center)
detection.false_positive_notes: Not all old buildings are designated historic. A building must be formally qualified as historic to use alternative compliance. Modern buildings designed to look historic do not qualify. Some historic buildings have made full ADA compliance without alternative methods.

cbc_stricter_slope_violation.title: CBC Stricter Slope Requirement Violation
cbc_stricter_slope_violation.description: Ramp meets federal ADA standards but fails California's stricter CBC slope requirements for existing buildings.

federal_ada.title: Ramp Slope
federal_ada.requirement: ADA allows existing ramps to have steeper slopes in limited situations: 1:10 maximum for 6-inch maximum rise, 1:8 maximum for 3-inch maximum rise (existing buildings only).

cbc_title24.title: Slope
cbc_title24.requirement: CBC does NOT allow the ADA exceptions for steeper slopes in existing buildings. All ramps in California must meet the 1:12 maximum running slope regardless of when they were constructed. This makes CBC significantly stricter for existing buildings.

cbc_stricter_slope_violation.legal_risk: In California, meeting ADA but not CBC creates full legal exposure under the Unruh Act ($4,000+ per occurrence). Courts apply the stricter CBC standard. Business owners who believe their ramp is 'ADA compliant' may be surprised by California's stricter requirements.
cbc_stricter_slope_violation.cost_note: SF market rate for rebuilding a ramp to meet CBC 1:12 standard when existing ramp uses ADA's steeper-slope exception. Often requires extending ramp length, which may involve significant site work.

remediation.summary: Rebuild ramp to meet CBC's strict 1:12 maximum slope requirement without ADA exceptions.
steps: 1. Measure existing ramp slope to determine current grade
2. Calculate required run length at 1:12 ratio for the total rise
3. Design new ramp layout accommodating longer run (may require switchback)
4. Apply for SF DBI alterations permit
5. Demolish existing non-compliant ramp
6. Construct new ramp at 1:12 maximum slope with all required features
7. Install compliant handrails, landings, and edge protection
8. Schedule final inspection

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction.

visual_cues: Existing ramp that appears moderately steep (steeper than 1:12 but not extreme)
Short ramp covering noticeable height change
Ramp at existing/older building that may have been built to older ADA exception standards
common_contexts: Older buildings with ramps built before CBC tightened requirements
Ramps designed by out-of-state firms applying federal ADA only
Retrofits that used ADA's existing building exceptions
detection.false_positive_notes: Ramps at or below 1:12 (8.33%) comply with both ADA and CBC. This violation is specifically about ramps between 1:12 and 1:8 slope that would pass ADA but fail CBC. Precise measurement is essential — visual assessment cannot reliably distinguish 1:12 from 1:10 slope.

tenant_improvement_trigger.title: Tenant Improvement Accessibility Trigger
tenant_improvement_trigger.description: Tenant improvement project triggers mandatory accessibility upgrades under CBC path of travel requirements.

federal_ada.title: Alterations: Path of Travel
federal_ada.requirement: When alterations affect usability of a primary function area, the path of travel must be made accessible. Cost limited to 20% of alteration cost.

cbc_title24.title: Path of Travel Requirements
cbc_title24.requirement: Tenant improvements (TIs) that alter primary function areas trigger path of travel upgrades. The scope includes: primary entrance, route to altered area, restrooms serving the area, drinking fountains, and signage. The 20% cost cap applies, but projects exceeding the valuation threshold may require full compliance.

sf_local.title: Tenant Improvement Accessibility Review
sf_local.requirement: SF DBI reviews all TI permit applications for accessibility trigger compliance. Plans must show path of travel upgrades or provide cost documentation for the 20% limitation. CASp review recommended for complex projects.

tenant_improvement_trigger.legal_risk: Completing a TI without required accessibility upgrades violates CBC and exposes both tenant and landlord to Unruh Act litigation ($4,000+ per occurrence). SF DBI may withhold certificate of occupancy. Future plaintiffs can point to the TI as evidence that accessibility should have been addressed.
tenant_improvement_trigger.cost_note: SF market rate for path of travel upgrades triggered by a TI project. Typically 10-20% of the TI construction budget. Includes entrance, route, restroom, and signage improvements within the 20% cost cap.

remediation.summary: Include required accessibility upgrades in tenant improvement scope per CBC path of travel requirements.
steps: 1. Calculate adjusted construction cost of TI project
2. Determine if project alters a primary function area (triggers path of travel)
3. Calculate 20% budget for accessibility upgrades
4. Prioritize upgrades per CBC priority order: entrance, route, restrooms, other
5. If budget exceeds 20%: document with SF DBI for disproportionate cost exemption
6. Include accessibility scope in TI permit application
7. Coordinate accessibility work with TI construction schedule
8. Schedule CASp inspection for compliance verification
9. Complete work before requesting certificate of occupancy
remediation.contractor_type: Licensed general contractor with CASp consultant
remediation.permit_type: SF DBI Building Permit (combined with TI permit)

tax_credits.notes: Eligible for IRS Section 44 Disabled Access Credit and Section 190 Barrier Removal Deduction. Accessibility costs are part of the TI cost basis for depreciation.

visual_cues: Recently renovated interior with outdated/inaccessible entrance
New storefront build-out in older building shell
Modern fixtures inside with original non-compliant doors/ramps
Construction permits visible for interior renovation
common_contexts: Restaurant build-outs in existing retail spaces
Medical/dental office fit-outs in commercial buildings
detection.false_positive_notes: Not all interior work triggers path of travel requirements. Cosmetic changes (paint, wallpaper, carpet) are typically not alterations affecting primary function areas. Mechanical, electrical, or plumbing work in non-public areas may not trigger requirements.
